EXT. CITY STREETS - CHRISTMAS MORNING

The people shovelling on the housetops are jovial and full of glee, calling out from the parapets and now and then exchanging a facetious snowball—laughing heartily if it goes right and not less heartily if it goes wrong.

The poulterers' shops are still half open. The fruiterers' are radiant: great round pot-bellied baskets of chestnuts loll at the doors; ruddy Spanish Onions shine on the shelves; pears and apples cluster in blooming pyramids; bunches of grapes dangle from hooks; piles of filberts, Norfolk Biffins, oranges and lemons crowd the windows. Gold and silver fish gasp round and round a bowl set among the fruit.

Through the gaps in the Grocers' shutters: scales descend with a merry sound; twine and roller part briskly; canisters rattle; the blended scents of tea and coffee pour into the street. Customers tumble against each other at the door, crash their wicker baskets, leave purchases and run back for them, all in the best humour possible. The Grocer and his people fasten their aprons with polished hearts worn outside.

Steeples begin to call. Good people flock through the streets in their best clothes and gayest faces. From bye-streets and nameless turnings, innumerable poor people carry their dinners toward the bakers' shops.

INT. BAKER'S DOORWAY - DAY

The GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PRESENT stands with SCROOGE beside him in a baker's doorway. The Spirit is gigantic, bare-chested and robed in deep green bordered with white fur, dark brown curls free, holly crown upon his head, torch like a Mighty Blaze in one hand. He takes off the covers as the bearers pass and sprinkles incense on their dinners from his torch.

Once or twice, when angry words rise between dinner-carriers who have jostled each other, he sheds a few drops of water on them from the torch. Their good humour is restored directly.

DINNER-CARRIER
It was a shame to quarrel upon Christmas Day.

ANOTHER DINNER-CARRIER
And so it was! God love it, so it was!

In time the bells cease. The bakers shut up. A genial shadowing forth of all these dinners remains in the thawed blotch of wet above each baker's oven; the pavement smokes as if its stones were cooking too.

SCROOGE
Is there a peculiar flavour in what you sprinkle from your torch?

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PRESENT
There is. My own.

SCROOGE
Would it apply to any kind of dinner on this day?

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PRESENT
To any kindly given. To a poor one most.

SCROOGE
Why to a poor one most?

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PRESENT
Because it needs it most.

SCROOGE
(after a moment's thought)
Spirit, I wonder you, of all the beings in the many worlds about us, should desire to cramp these people's opportunities of innocent enjoyment.

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PRESENT
I!

SCROOGE
You would deprive them of their means of dining every seventh day, often the only day on which they can be said to dine at all. Wouldn't you?

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PRESENT
I!

SCROOGE
You seek to close these places on the Seventh Day? And it comes to the same thing.

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PRESENT
I seek!

SCROOGE
Forgive me if I am wrong. It has been done in your name, or at least in that of your family.

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PRESENT
There are some upon this earth of yours who lay claim to know us, and who do their deeds of passion, pride, ill-will, hatred, envy, bigotry, and selfishness in our name, who are as strange to us and all our kith and kin, as if they had never lived. Remember that, and charge their doings on themselves, not us.

Scrooge promises that he will. They go on, invisible, into the suburbs. Notwithstanding his gigantic size, the Ghost accommodates himself to any place with ease.

EXT. CRATCHIT DOORSTEP - DAY

The Spirit leads Scrooge straight to a humble four-roomed house. On the threshold he smiles and stops to bless Bob Cratchit's dwelling with a sprinkling of his torch.

INT. CRATCHIT SITTING ROOM - DAY

A poor but brave little room. MRS. CRATCHIT rises—dressed poorly in a twice-turned gown, but brave in ribbons which make a goodly show for sixpence. She lays the cloth, assisted by BELINDA CRATCHIT, second of her daughters, also brave in ribbons.

PETER CRATCHIT plunges a fork into the saucepan of potatoes. The corners of his monstrous shirt collar (Bob's private property, conferred upon his son and heir in honour of the day) get into his mouth; he rejoices to find himself so gallantly attired.

Two smaller Cratchits, LITTLE BOY CRATCHIT and LITTLE GIRL CRATCHIT, come tearing in.

LITTLE BOY CRATCHIT
Outside the baker's we smelt the goose!

LITTLE GIRL CRATCHIT
And knew it for our own!

They dance about the table in luxurious thoughts of sage and onion, and exalt Peter to the skies. Peter, not proud although his collars nearly choke him, blows the fire until the slow potatoes bubble up and knock loudly at the saucepan-lid.

MRS. CRATCHIT
What has ever got your precious father then? And your brother, Tiny Tim! And Martha warn't as late last Christmas Day by half-an-hour?

MARTHA CRATCHIT appears in the doorway, a poor apprentice girl in plain clothes, shawl and bonnet.

MARTHA CRATCHIT
Here's Martha, mother!

LITTLE BOY CRATCHIT
Here's Martha, mother!

LITTLE GIRL CRATCHIT
Hurrah! There's such a goose, Martha!

MRS. CRATCHIT
(kissing her a dozen times, taking off her shawl and bonnet)
Why, bless your heart alive, my dear, how late you are!

MARTHA CRATCHIT
We'd a deal of work to finish up last night, and had to clear away this morning, mother!

MRS. CRATCHIT
Well! Never mind so long as you are come. Sit ye down before the fire, my dear, and have a warm, Lord bless ye!

LITTLE BOY CRATCHIT
No, no! There's father coming!

LITTLE GIRL CRATCHIT
Hide, Martha, hide!

Martha hides behind the closet door.

In comes BOB CRATCHIT—little, threadbare clothes darned up and brushed to look seasonable, at least three feet of comforter exclusive of the fringe hanging down before him—with TINY TIM upon his shoulder. Tiny Tim bears a little crutch; his limbs are supported by an iron frame. He is very small, pale, bright-eyed.

BOB CRATCHIT
(looking round)
Why, where's our Martha?

MRS. CRATCHIT
Not coming.

BOB CRATCHIT
(sudden declension in his high spirits)
Not coming? Not coming upon Christmas Day!

Martha cannot bear it. She comes out prematurely and runs into his arms. The two young Cratchits hustle Tiny Tim off toward the wash-house.

LITTLE BOY CRATCHIT
Come and hear the pudding singing in the copper!

MRS. CRATCHIT
(rallying Bob)
And how did little Tim behave?

BOB CRATCHIT
As good as gold, and better. Somehow he gets thoughtful, sitting by himself so much, and thinks the strangest things you ever heard. He told me, coming home, that he hoped the people saw him in the church, because he was a cripple, and it might be pleasant to them to remember upon Christmas Day, who made lame beggars walk, and blind men see.

Bob's voice is tremulous. It trembles more when he adds—

BOB CRATCHIT
Tiny Tim was growing strong and hearty.

Tiny Tim's active little crutch is heard upon the floor. Back he comes, escorted by his brother and sister, to his stool before the fire.

Bob turns up his cuffs—as if they were capable of being made more shabby—and compounds some hot mixture in a jug with gin and lemons, stirs it round and round, and puts it on the hob to simmer.

Peter and the two young Cratchits go to fetch the goose. They return in high procession.

Such a bustle ensues that a goose might be the rarest of all birds. Mrs. Cratchit makes the gravy (ready beforehand in a little saucepan) hissing hot. Peter mashes the potatoes with incredible vigour. Belinda sweetens up the apple-sauce. Martha dusts the hot plates. Bob takes Tiny Tim beside him in a tiny corner at the table. The two young Cratchits set chairs for everybody, not forgetting themselves, and mount guard upon their posts, cramming spoons into their mouths lest they shriek for goose before their turn.

The dishes are set on. Grace is said. A breathless pause. Mrs. Cratchit looks slowly all along the carving-knife, then plunges it in the breast. The long-expected gush of stuffing issues forth. One murmur of delight arises all round the board. Tiny Tim, excited by the two young Cratchits, beats on the table with the handle of his knife.

TINY TIM
Hurrah!

BOB CRATCHIT
I don't believe there ever was such a goose cooked!

Its tenderness and flavour, size and cheapness, are the themes of universal admiration. Eked out by apple-sauce and mashed potatoes, it is a sufficient dinner for the whole family. Mrs. Cratchit surveys one small atom of a bone upon the dish with great delight.

MRS. CRATCHIT
We haven't ate it all at last!

Yet every one has had enough. The youngest Cratchits in particular are steeped in sage and onion to the eyebrows.

The plates being changed by Belinda, Mrs. Cratchit leaves the room alone—too nervous to bear witnesses—to take the pudding up.

INT. CRATCHIT WASH-HOUSE - DAY

Steam. A smell like a washing-day—that is the cloth. A smell like an eating-house and a pastrycook's next door to each other, with a laundress's next door to that—that is the pudding.

INT. CRATCHIT SITTING ROOM - DAY

In half a minute Mrs. Cratchit enters—flushed, but smiling proudly—with the pudding, like a speckled cannon-ball, so hard and firm, blazing in half of half-a-quartern of ignited brandy, and bedight with Christmas holly stuck into the top.

BOB CRATCHIT
(calmly)
I regard it as the greatest success achieved by Mrs. Cratchit since our marriage.

MRS. CRATCHIT
Now the weight is off my mind, I will confess I had my doubts about the quantity of flour.

Everybody has something to say about it. Nobody says or thinks it is at all a small pudding for a large family. Any Cratchit would blush to hint at such a thing.

At last the dinner is all done, the cloth cleared, the hearth swept, the fire made up. The compound in the jug is tasted and considered perfect. Apples and oranges are put upon the table, and a shovelful of chestnuts on the fire.

All the Cratchit family draw round the hearth in what Bob calls a circle, meaning half a one. At Bob's elbow stands the family display of glass: two tumblers, and a custard-cup without a handle. These hold the hot stuff from the jug as well as golden goblets would have done. Bob serves it out with beaming looks. The chestnuts on the fire sputter and crack noisily.

BOB CRATCHIT
A Merry Christmas to us all, my dears. God bless us!

ALL CRATCHITS
God bless us!

TINY TIM
(the last of all)
God bless us every one!

He sits very close to his father's side upon his little stool. Bob holds his withered little hand as if he loved the child, and wished to keep him by his side, and dreaded that he might be taken from him.

Scrooge, invisible beside the Ghost, watches with an interest he has never felt before.

SCROOGE
Spirit, tell me if Tiny Tim will live.

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PRESENT
I see a vacant seat in the poor chimney-corner, and a crutch without an owner, carefully preserved. If these shadows remain unaltered by the Future, the child will die.

SCROOGE
No, no. Oh, no, kind Spirit! say he will be spared.

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PRESENT
If these shadows remain unaltered by the Future, none other of my race will find him here. What then? If he be like to die, he had better do it, and decrease the surplus population.

Scrooge hangs his head to hear his own words quoted by the Spirit, and is overcome with penitence and grief.

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PRESENT
Man, if man you be in heart, not adamant, forbear that wicked cant until you have discovered What the surplus is, and Where it is. Will you decide what men shall live, what men shall die? It may be, that in the sight of Heaven, you are more worthless and less fit to live than millions like this poor man's child. Oh God! to hear the Insect on the leaf pronouncing on the too much life among his hungry brothers in the dust!

Scrooge bends before the Ghost's rebuke, trembling, eyes upon the ground. He raises them speedily on hearing his own name.

BOB C

===== FOUNTAIN_PART 4/5 =====
EXT. OPEN PLACE - CONTINUOUS

The GHOST OF CHRISTMAS YET TO COME answers not, but points onward with its hand. It is tall, stately, shrouded in a deep black garment that conceals head, face, and form — nothing visible save one outstretched hand.

SCROOGE
You are about to show me shadows of the things that have not happened, but will happen in the time before us. Is that so, Spirit?

The upper portion of the garment contracts for an instant in its folds, as if the Spirit has inclined its head. That is the only answer.

Scrooge's legs tremble. He can hardly stand. The Spirit pauses, observing his condition, giving him time to recover. Behind the dusky shroud, unseen eyes seem intently fixed upon him. He sees nothing but a spectral hand and one great heap of black.

SCROOGE
Ghost of the Future! I fear you more than any spectre I have seen. But as I know your purpose is to do me good, and as I hope to live to be another man from what I was, I am prepared to bear you company, and do it with a thankful heart. Will you not speak to me?

No reply. The hand points straight before them.

SCROOGE
Lead on! Lead on! The night is waning fast, and it is precious time to me, I know. Lead on, Spirit!

The Phantom moves away as it came. Scrooge follows in the shadow of its dress, which bears him up and carries him along.

EXT. THE EXCHANGE - DAY

The city springs up about them. They stand in the heart of it, on 'Change, amongst the merchants — hurrying, chinking money in their pockets, conversing in groups, looking at watches, trifling with great gold seals.

The Spirit stops beside one little knot of business men. The hand points to them. Scrooge advances to listen.

FAT MERCHANT
(great fat man, monstrous chin)
No, I don't know much about it, either way. I only know he's dead.

SECOND MERCHANT
When did he die?

FAT MERCHANT
Last night, I believe.

THIRD MERCHANT
(taking snuff from a vast snuff-box)
Why, what was the matter with him? I thought he'd never die.

FAT MERCHANT
(with a yawn)
God knows.

RED-FACED GENTLEMAN
(pendulous excrescence on his nose that shakes like a turkey-cock's gills)
What has he done with his money?

FAT MERCHANT
(yawning again)
I haven't heard. Left it to his company, perhaps. He hasn't left it to me. That's all I know.

A general laugh.

FAT MERCHANT
It's likely to be a very cheap funeral, for upon my life I don't know of anybody to go to it. Suppose we make up a party and volunteer?

RED-FACED GENTLEMAN
I don't mind going if a lunch is provided. But I must be fed, if I make one.

Another laugh.

FAT MERCHANT
Well, I am the most disinterested among you, after all, for I never wear black gloves, and I never eat lunch. But I'll offer to go, if anybody else will. When I come to think of it, I'm not at all sure that I wasn't his most particular friend; for we used to stop and speak whenever we met. Bye, bye!

Speakers and listeners stroll away and mix with other groups. Scrooge knows the men. He looks toward the Spirit for an explanation. The Phantom glides on.

EXT. CITY STREET - DAY

The Spirit's finger points to two persons meeting. Scrooge knows these men perfectly — wealthy, of great importance. He has always stood well in their esteem, strictly in a business point of view.

FIRST BUSINESSMAN
How are you?

SECOND BUSINESSMAN
How are you?

FIRST BUSINESSMAN
Well! Old Scratch has got his own at last, hey?

SECOND BUSINESSMAN
So I am told. Cold, isn't it?

FIRST BUSINESSMAN
Seasonable for Christmas time. You're not a skater, I suppose?

SECOND BUSINESSMAN
No. No. Something else to think of. Good morning!

Not another word. That is their meeting, their conversation, and their parting.

Scrooge looks about for his own image in his accustomed corner. Another man stands there. The clock points to his usual time of day. He sees no likeness of himself among the multitudes. Quiet and dark, beside him stands the Phantom, with its outstretched hand. Scrooge shudders and feels very cold.

EXT. OBSCURE STREET - NIGHT

They leave the busy scene for an obscure part of the town. The ways are foul and narrow; the shops and houses wretched; the people half-naked, drunken, slipshod, ugly. Alleys and archways disgorge smell, dirt, and life upon the straggling streets. The whole quarter reeks with crime, filth, and misery.

Far in this den stands a low-browed, beetling shop below a pent-house roof — iron, old rags, bottles, bones, and greasy offal bought and sold.

INT. OLD JOE'S SHOP - NIGHT

Upon the floor: heaps of rusty keys, nails, chains, hinges, files, scales, weights, refuse iron. Mountains of unseemly rags, masses of corrupted fat, sepulchres of bones.

OLD JOE, grey-haired, nearly seventy, sits among the wares by a charcoal stove of old bricks, screened from the cold by a frousy curtaining of miscellaneous tatters hung upon a line. He smokes his pipe in calm retirement.

Scrooge and the Phantom enter just as a woman with a heavy bundle slinks in — the CHARWOMAN. Scarcely has she entered when MRS. DILBER, similarly laden, comes in too, closely followed by the UNDERTAKER'S MAN in faded black. All three startle at the sight of each other. After blank astonishment — in which Old Joe joins — they burst into a laugh.

CHARWOMAN
Let the charwoman alone to be the first! Let the laundress alone to be the second; and let the undertaker's man alone to be the third. Look here, old Joe, here's a chance! If we haven't all three met here without meaning it!

OLD JOE
(removing his pipe)
You couldn't have met in a better place. Come into the parlour. You were made free of it long ago, you know; and the other two an't strangers. Stop till I shut the door of the shop. Ah! How it skreeks! There an't such a rusty bit of metal in the place as its own hinges, I believe; and I'm sure there's no such old bones here, as mine. Ha, ha! We're all suitable to our calling, we're well matched. Come into the parlour. Come into the parlour.

The parlour is the space behind the screen of rags. Old Joe rakes the fire with an old stair-rod and trims his smoky lamp with the stem of his pipe.

The Charwoman throws her bundle on the floor and sits on a stool in a flaunting manner, crossing her elbows on her knees, bold defiance at the other two.

CHARWOMAN
What odds then! What odds, Mrs. Dilber? Every person has a right to take care of themselves. He always did.

MRS. DILBER
That's true, indeed! No man more so.

CHARWOMAN
Why then, don't stand staring as if you was afraid, woman; who's the wiser? We're not going to pick holes in each other's coats, I suppose?

MRS. DILBER
No, indeed!

UNDERTAKER'S MAN
We should hope not.

CHARWOMAN
Very well, then! That's enough. Who's the worse for the loss of a few things like these? Not a dead man, I suppose.

MRS. DILBER
(laughing)
No, indeed.

CHARWOMAN
If he wanted to keep 'em after he was dead, a wicked old screw, why wasn't he natural in his lifetime? If he had been, he'd have had somebody to look after him when he was struck with Death, instead of lying gasping out his last there, alone by himself.

MRS. DILBER
It's the truest word that ever was spoke. It's a judgment on him.

CHARWOMAN
I wish it was a little heavier judgment, and it should have been, you may depend upon it, if I could have laid my hands on anything else. Open that bundle, old Joe, and let me know the value of it. Speak out plain. I'm not afraid to be the first, nor afraid for them to see it. We know pretty well that we were helping ourselves, before we met here, I believe. It's no sin. Open the bundle, Joe.

But the man in faded black mounts the breach first and produces his plunder: a seal or two, a pencil-case, a pair of sleeve-buttons, and a brooch of no great value. Old Joe examines and appraises each, chalking sums upon the wall, then adds them up.

OLD JOE
That's your account, and I wouldn't give another sixpence, if I was to be boiled for not doing it. Who's next?

Mrs. Dilber is next. Sheets and towels, a little wearing apparel, two old-fashioned silver teaspoons, a pair of sugar-tongs, and a few boots. Her account goes on the wall.

OLD JOE
I always give too much to ladies. It's a weakness of mine, and that's the way I ruin myself. That's your account. If you asked me for another penny, and made it an open question, I'd repent of being so liberal and knock off half-a-crown.

CHARWOMAN
And now undo my bundle, Joe.

Joe goes down on his knees, unfastens a great many knots, and drags out a large and heavy roll of dark stuff.

OLD JOE
What do you call this? Bed-curtains!

CHARWOMAN
(laughing, leaning forward on her crossed arms)
Ah! Bed-curtains!

OLD JOE
You don't mean to say you took 'em down, rings and all, with him lying there?

CHARWOMAN
Yes I do. Why not?

OLD JOE
You were born to make your fortune, and you'll certainly do it.

CHARWOMAN
(coolly)
I certainly shan't hold my hand, when I can get anything in it by reaching it out, for the sake of such a man as He was, I promise you, Joe. Don't drop that oil upon the blankets, now.

OLD JOE
His blankets?

CHARWOMAN
Whose else's do you think? He isn't likely to take cold without 'em, I dare say.

OLD JOE
(stopping, looking up)
I hope he didn't die of anything catching? Eh?

CHARWOMAN
Don't you be afraid of that. I an't so fond of his company that I'd loiter about him for such things, if he did. Ah! you may look through that shirt till your eyes ache; but you won't find a hole in it, nor a threadbare place. It's the best he had, and a fine one too. They'd have wasted it, if it hadn't been for me.

OLD JOE
What do you call wasting of it?

CHARWOMAN
(with a laugh)
Putting it on him to be buried in, to be sure. Somebody was fool enough to do it, but I took it off again. If calico an't good enough for such a purpose, it isn't good enough for anything. It's quite as becoming to the body. He can't look uglier than he did in that one.

Scrooge listens in horror. Grouped about their spoil in the scanty lamp-light, he views them with detestation and disgust.

Old Joe produces a flannel bag with money and tells out their several gains upon the ground.

CHARWOMAN
Ha, ha! This is the end of it, you see! He frightened every one away from him when he was alive, to profit us when he was dead! Ha, ha, ha!

SCROOGE
(shuddering from head to foot)
Spirit! I see, I see. The case of this unhappy man might be my own. My life tends that way, now. Merciful Heaven, what is this!

INT. DARK BEDCHAMBER - NIGHT

The scene has changed. Scrooge almost touches a bed: a bare, uncurtained bed. Beneath a ragged sheet lies a something covered up which, though dumb, announces itself in awful language.

The room is very dark. A pale light, rising in the outer air, falls straight upon the bed; and on it, plundered and bereft, unwatched, unwept, uncared for, is the body of this man.

The Phantom's steady hand points to the head. The cover is so carelessly adjusted that the slightest raising of it would disclose the face. Scrooge longs to do it, but has no power to withdraw the veil.

A cat tears at the door. There is a sound of gnawing rats beneath the hearth-stone.

SCROOGE
Spirit! this is a fearful place. In leaving it, I shall not leave its lesson, trust me. Let us go!

Still the Ghost points with an unmoved finger to the head.

SCROOGE
I understand you, and I would do it, if I could. But I have not the power, Spirit. I have not the power.

Again it seems to look upon him.

SCROOGE
(agonised)
If there is any person in the town, who feels emotion caused by this man's death, show that person to me, Spirit, I beseech you!

The Phantom spreads its dark robe before him for a moment, like a wing.

INT. DEBTORS' PARLOUR - DAY

Withdrawing the robe reveals a room by daylight. CAROLINE, a mild and patient young mother, walks up and down with anxious eagerness. She starts at every sound, looks out from the window, glances at the clock, tries in vain to work with her needle, and can hardly bear the voices of her CHILDREN in their play.

At length the long-expected knock. She hurries to the do

[[… partial truncated for merge prompt …]]

===== FOUNTAIN_PART 5/5 =====
EXT. LONDON STREETS - DAY

Snow lies clean on the cobbles. Months have softened into a bright winter morning. SCROOGE, older coat brushed, face open and alive, walks among the PASSERSBY with a step that belongs to the living. He lifts his hat. He greets a child. He stops a beggar with a coin and a clasp of the hand.

NARRATOR (V.O.)
Scrooge was better than his word. He did it all, and infinitely more.

A knot of merchants watch him pass. One chuckles behind a glove. Scrooge only smiles wider and goes on.

NARRATOR (V.O.)
Some people laughed to see the alteration in him, but he let them laugh, and little heeded them; for he was wise enough to know that nothing ever happened on this globe, for good, at which some people did not have their fill of laughter in the outset. His own heart laughed: and that was quite enough for him.

INT. CRATCHIT HOME - DAY

A small room made warm by a good fire and a second coal-scuttle. BOB CRATCHIT at the table; MRS. CRATCHIT laying bread; PETER CRATCHIT, MARTHA CRATCHIT, LITTLE GIRL CRATCHIT, LITTLE BOY CRATCHIT crowded near. TINY TIM, living, bright-eyed, his little crutch against the chair, sits upon SCROOGE'S knee as upon a second father's.

Scrooge steadies the boy. Tim leans into him without fear.

NARRATOR (V.O.)
And to Tiny Tim, who did NOT die, he was a second father. He became as good a friend, as good a master, and as good a man, as the good old city knew, or any other good old city, town, or borough, in the good old world.

Bob meets his wife's eyes across the room. Both shine.

EXT. LONDON STREETS - CHRISTMAS MORNING

Church bells. SCROOGE moves through the throng, wishing strangers joy, stopping to watch carolers, pressing money into the PORTLY GENTLEMAN's hand with both of his when their paths cross again. No Spirit walks beside him. Only daylight, and the city, and his own changed gait.

NARRATOR (V.O.)
He had no further intercourse with Spirits, but lived upon the Total Abstinence Principle, ever afterwards; and it was always said of him, that he knew how to keep Christmas well, if any man alive possessed the knowledge.

INT. CRATCHIT HOME - CHRISTMAS DAY

The Cratchit table is fuller than before. SCROOGE at its head as welcome guest; BOB CRATCHIT carving; MRS. CRATCHIT flushed with pride; the children noisy and glad. TINY TIM raised in Bob's arms for the toast, crutch tucked close.

NARRATOR (V.O.)
May that be truly said of us, and all of us!

TINY TIM
God bless us, every one!

Scrooge's face breaks into pure delight. The family echoes the boy. Outside the window, snow falls soft on London.

FADE OUT.

THE END

===== END PARTS =====
Return the merged Fountain screenplay only.

systemPrompt:
Act as an expert screenwriter adapting a book into an editable Fountain 1.1 screenplay.

This is the operator-facing screenplay. Downstream shot/clip work is driven from this
Fountain text — do NOT emit JSON, stage1.v1, story_beats arrays, or schema fields.

================================================================
GOALS (narrative)
================================================================
1. Cover the book’s essential story in roughly 180 minutes
   of finished film. Keep the adaptation tight; do not pad.
2. Break the story into scenes a director can schedule: one clear location + time + purpose.
3. Inside each scene, write ordered concrete events we must SEE or HEAR (action + dialogue).
4. Keep a closed cast: every speaker is a named CHARACTER (ALL CAPS). No “MAN #2” ghosts.
   Protagonists with no given name in the book still get a stable token (period-fitting name,
   or THE NARRATOR / THE WIFE — never bare THE WOMAN / THE MAN as the hero cue).
5. Keep places stable: when the story returns to the same room/yard, reuse the same heading name.
6. Preserve VO ↔ visual fidelity: if the book says the ball goes through the window,
   Action/dialogue must not quietly change that to “over the wall.”

PRIORITY IF CONFLICTS:
1. Valid Fountain screenplay (parseable headings, characters, dialogue)
2. Narrative fidelity to the book
3. Runtime band (short and filmable)
4. Closed cast + consistent looks/places

================================================================
SOURCE TEXT HANDLING
================================================================
- Skip Project Gutenberg license/boilerplate, tables of contents, and end matter.
  Start from the real story (title/author if present, then chapter 1 / opening scene).
- If the input is ALREADY a Fountain screenplay (Title: / INT. / CHARACTER cues),
  clean and normalize it into valid Fountain — do not invent a different plot.
- If the text is truncated or split with markers like
  `[[… middle of book omitted for length …]]`, treat each block as part of ONE story.
  Cover the full arc present across opening + middle + ending excerpts
  (setup → crisis/turn → resolution). Do not invent chapters that are not present.
  Do not stop after the opening act when later excerpts are provided.

================================================================
OUTPUT FORMAT (HARD) — FOUNTAIN ONLY
================================================================
Return ONLY a complete Fountain 1.1 plain-text screenplay.
No markdown fences. No commentary before or after.

TITLE PAGE (Fountain keys, first lines):
Title: …
Credit: Written by
Author: … (from book if known)
Source: Adapted from book
Draft date: M/D/YYYY

Then blank line, then scenes.

SCENE HEADINGS:
- INT. or EXT. (or EST. / I/E.) + real location + time
  Good: INT. LIVING ROOM - NIGHT
  Good: EXT. BACKYARD - DAY
  Good: EXT. CASTLE COURTYARD - NIGHT
  Good: INT. HALL AND STAIRS - NIGHT
  Good: INT. HALL AND SITTING ROOM - NIGHT
  Bad: INT. STORY - PAGE 3
  Bad: INT. SCENE - DAY
  Bad: INT. CHAPTER 1
  Bad: INT. HOUSE - VARIOUS - NIGHT
  Bad: INT. HOUSE - VARIOUS ROOMS - NIGHT
  Bad: INT. MULTIPLE LOCATIONS - NIGHT
  Bad: INT. SEVERAL ROOMS - NIGHT
  Bad: INT. ELSEWHERE - DAY
- HARD: every heading must name a concrete, filmable place a crew can light and dress.
  Never put multi-place filler in the heading (VARIOUS, VARIOUS ROOMS, MULTIPLE, SEVERAL,
  ELSEWHERE, DIFFERENT ROOMS/PLACES/LOCATIONS, AROUND THE HOUSE, THROUGHOUT…).
  A house tour is NOT "VARIOUS ROOMS" — name 1–2 rooms or fold the walk into Action.
- Do NOT put PAGE or CHAPTER in the scene heading line itself.
- Prefer concrete places from the book (COUNTING HOUSE, SHIP DECK, NURSERY, …).

Do NOT put page numbers in the screenplay (no `= page N`, no `[[page N]]`, no PAGE in headings).
Book fidelity is checked in the UI by matching scene text to the prepared book — not via page tags.

BODY:
- Action lines = what we SEE (story beats / visual events)
- Third-person narration / storybook voice → NARRATOR (character cue) + dialogue lines
- Spoken book lines → CHARACTER (ALL CAPS) + dialogue under them
- Parentheticals sparingly when delivery matters
- Dual dialogue: caret ^ on the second speaker only when simultaneous
- Recurring address / commentary speakers (first-person confessor, storybook NARRATOR,
  diarist, guide, chorus): do not default every reflective line to V.O. over the action.
  If you establish an on-camera "frame" early (a room, chair, desk, or stage position where
  they speak to camera or an implied listener), reuse that same heading as a cutback
  through the film for some of their commentary — especially at major turns (setup,
  escalation, crisis/peak, aftermath). Keep V.O. when the words must ride over
  simultaneous action that we need to see (the key prop, the blow, the chase, the reveal).
  Do not leave an entire peak stretch as only V.O. in one location if a frame already exists.
- If Action already visually shows what a V.O. line states (a reaction, a sound, a physical
  response), trim the redundant clause from the V.O. rather than restating it in both places.
  Storybook irony/theme may stay in V.O. only when it adds meaning Action cannot show —
  do not caption the same beat twice (Action + VO that only repeats it).
- When a single V.O. passage runs more than ~3 sentences over one static Action line,
  break it into two shorter V.O. beats with a one-line visual or sound micro-beat between
  them (a sound, a tremor, a silhouette) rather than one long block over one frozen image.
- Action describes what we SEE (props, faces, rooms, weather). Avoid modern currency notation
  in Action ($8, $20/week); show poverty or wealth visually. Keep exact money in dialogue
  or brief NARRATOR lines only when the book’s wording needs it.

DIALOGUE FIDELITY (HARD — book wording over paraphrase):
- Prefer the book’s actual spoken words. Do not modernize, soften, or “movie-ize” dialogue.
- Iconic / famous lines must stay close to the book (wording and cadence), e.g.:
  Scrooge “Bah! Humbug!”, “Are there no prisons?”, “decrease the surplus population”,
  Tiny Tim “God bless us, every one!”, Alice “Curiouser and curiouser!”,
  Dracula “Welcome to my house…”, Tell-Tale Heart opening “True! — nervous — very, very dreadfully nervous…”,
  Raven refrain “Nevermore”, Magi / Jungle Book set pieces, etc.
- First-person monologues and verse: keep the book’s voice; compress by cutting
  secondary lines, not by rewriting the narrator into generic modern speech.
- When runtime forces cuts: drop whole minor beats; do not paraphrase the lines you keep.
- Narration in the book that is clearly spoken or recited on-camera may become dialogue;
  pure prose description stays Action or NARRATOR VO — still use book phrases when VO quotes the text.

Do NOT invent major plot. Light visual connective tissue is OK.
Skip pure illustration / blank pages as their own scenes.
Do not turn every short paragraph into its own scene — group by location + purpose.

================================================================
CAST LOOKS & VOICES
================================================================
You are not writing JSON seeds, but the screenplay must lock identity in Action so
later shots stay consistent:

- Filmable identity only: species/age/build/hair/face/wardrobe + stable colors/markings
  that must not drift.
- Signature props/wardrobe the book establishes must stay on when those characters re-enter.
- One render medium for the whole film (period live-action / gothic / stylized illustration / …).
  State medium once early (title-page Notes: or first Action) if helpful.
  Humans and animals share one medium — never photoreal people beside cartoon animals
  unless the whole piece is live-action.
- Nicknames / epithets belong in dialogue or titles, NOT as physical description.
- Metaphorical wardrobe: if text is figurative, do not invent garments the story never shows.
- Exclusive OR in the book: pick ONE for that beat.
- Pure voice-only narrator: NARRATOR dialogue; do not force a face on screen.
- Recurring speakers use the same ALL-CAPS token every time (SCROOGE, ALICE, DELLA, …).
- Supporting roles with dialogue (officers, guards, servants, neighbors): give each a proper
  ALL-CAPS name token (OFFICER REYNOLDS, MRS. CLEMM), not FIRST/SECOND/THIRD OFFICER,
  OFFICER 1, POLICE OFFICER #2. Numbers and ordinals are not stable cast keys.

================================================================
LOCATIONS
================================================================
- Use real, filmable place names grounded in the book.
- When the story returns to the same place, use the **exact same heading wording**
  (same location phrase every time — do not add/drop a building prefix like "OLD HOUSE -"
  on some visits and omit it on others).
- Multi-place story moments can be separate scenes (preferred) or brief transitions;
  do not invent new plot just to force a location change.
- Never use a vague placeholder as the location itself — including synonyms the model
  invents to dodge the ban: VARIOUS, VARIOUS ROOMS, MULTIPLE, MULTIPLE LOCATIONS,
  SEVERAL, SEVERAL ROOMS, ELSEWHERE, DIFFERENT ROOMS / PLACES / LOCATIONS,
  AROUND THE HOUSE, THROUGHOUT THE HOUSE. If a beat moves through several rooms with
  no dialogue, either name 1–2 concrete rooms (INT. HALL AND SITTING ROOM - NIGHT) or
  drop the heading and fold the movement into the Action of the scene it leads into.
- Epistolary / diary books: prefer dramatized scenes (people in places) over pure
  “INT. STUDY - DAY” letter-reading for every beat; a few journal VO moments are fine.

================================================================
FIDELITY, SPOILERS, DIALOGUE
================================================================
- Dialogue = book wording first. Paraphrase is a last resort when a line is too long for
  film time; never replace an iconic phrase with a modern rewrite.
- If the book withholds a given name or twist early, do not put it on screen or in dialogue
  before the book does. Never “ban” a secret by writing the secret string into the script
  as an instruction.
- “X said…” / internal thought in the book → NARRATOR VO unless the character is clearly
  speaking on camera. Keep distinctive book diction in that VO when it is quoting the text.
- Keep every speaker named; list recurring people consistently (same ALL-CAPS token).
- For classics (Scrooge, Alice, Frankenstein’s creature, Dracula, Della/Jim, Mowgli, Poe, …):
  hit the book’s iconic beats, turns, AND quotable lines; do not substitute a vague
  “similar” plot or softened dialogue.

================================================================
RUNTIME SHAPE
================================================================
Target about 180 minutes of finished film.
- Short stories / picture books: few scenes, dense beats, little padding.
  Aim roughly 8–18 scene headings total unless the story truly needs more.
- Novellas / novels: more scenes, still ONE coherent film — compress digression,
  keep the emotional/plot spine. Multi-chunk mode may send the book in ordered parts;
  when you see BOOK_CHUNK n/m, adapt only that part and honor continuity notes.
  Prefer ~15–40 scenes for a short-film cut of a long book, not one heading per paragraph.
Do not write one scene per chapter or paragraph unless location/purpose truly changes.
When the source is long and truncated in the prompt, cover only what is provided.
- Merge consecutive beats that share the same location and time of day into ONE scene
  with multiple Action/dialogue units — only split when place, time, or dramatic purpose
  clearly changes. Mark elapsed time in Action (“Two weeks later.”) rather than a dozen
  identical INT. ROOM - DAY headings.
- Montage of “hours” or “shopping” or travel: 2–3 specific micro-locations (or progressive
  Action in one place), not a single vague EXT. STREETS - DAY summary slug.
- Final thematic button: keep closing NARRATOR essay short (a few sentences) or prefer a
  silent/action image after the last plot line — do not stack a long VO sermon on top of
  a full visual resolution unless runtime allows.
- Compress secondary/connective beats first. When the book explicitly marks time
  passing at the story's central violent or emotional peak (the kill, the discovery,
  the confession) with language like "many minutes," "a long while," "hour after
  hour," give that moment its own tactile action beat rather than folding it into
  a single summary clause. One extra waiting gesture, one fading/growing sound, one
  physical detail there buys more of the book's intended effect per second than the
  same beat spent on connective tissue elsewhere. Cut runtime from the setup or the
  aftermath before you cut it from the peak.

================================================================
MULTI-CHUNK ADAPT (when BOOK_CHUNK n/m is in the user message)
================================================================
- Chunk 1/m: full title page + scenes for this part; establish cast tokens; no THE END.
- Chunk 2..m-1: NO title page; only new scenes; reuse CHARACTER tokens; no THE END.
- Chunk m/m: NO title page; finish the arc; include FADE OUT / THE END.
- Merge mode (if asked): one title page, consistent cast, full arc, no duplicate opens.

================================================================
OUTPUT
================================================================
Fountain only. No JSON. No trailing commentary.

================================================================
LEARNINGS (appended from Film Studio edit log)
================================================================

Rules:

Title: A Christmas Carol
Credit: Written by
Author: Charles Dickens
Source: Adapted from book
Draft date: 3/25/2025
Notes: Period live-action. Victorian London, Christmas 1843. Fog, frost, candlelight. Dickens's storybook voice as NARRATOR. Cast locked: EBENEZER SCROOGE (elderly, thin, pointed nose, shrivelled cheeks, red-rimmed eyes, thin blue lips, frosty rime on hair/brows/chin, dark tight coat, grating voice); JACOB MARLEY (dead partner; pigtail, waistcoat, tights, boots, transparent, steel chain of cash-boxes/keys/padlocks/ledgers/deeds/purses, kerchief bound about head and chin); FRED (Scrooge's nephew; young, ruddy, handsome, glowing from cold); BOB CRATCHIT (clerk; pinched, threadbare, long white comforter, no great-coat); MRS. CRATCHIT (brave in ribbons, twice-turned gown); TINY TIM (very small, pale, bright-eyed, little crutch, iron frame); MR. ALMSLEY and MR. WITHERS (portly pleasant charity gentlemen); GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PAST (childlike yet old, white hair, tunic of purest white, jet of light from head, extinguisher-cap); GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PRESENT (gigantic, green robe bordered with white fur, holly crown, glowing torch); GHOST OF CHRISTMAS YET TO COME (tall, stately, deep black shroud, one outstretched hand only).

===

FADE IN:

INT. REGISTER OFFICE - DAY

A heavy burial register lies open. A CLERGYMAN, a CLERK, an UNDERTAKER, and EBENEZER SCROOGE sign in turn. Scrooge's hand is steady, businesslike. Snow light at the window.

NARRATOR (V.O.)
Marley was dead: to begin with. There is no doubt whatever about that. The register of his burial was signed by the clergyman, the clerk, the undertaker, and the chief mourner. Scrooge signed it. Old Marley was as dead as a door-nail.

EXT. LONDON CHURCHYARD - DAY

A plain stone. Wind. Scrooge already turning away, hat low, counting nothing of grief.

NARRATOR (V.O.)
Scrooge knew he was dead? Of course he did. Scrooge and he were partners for I don't know how many years. Scrooge was his sole executor, his sole administrator, his sole friend, and sole mourner. And even Scrooge was not so dreadfully cut up by the sad event, but that he was an excellent man of business on the very day of the funeral, and solemnised it with an undoubted bargain.

EXT. SCROOGE AND MARLEY WAREHOUSE DOOR - DAY

Years later. Above the door, paint weathered but clear: SCROOGE AND MARLEY. Scrooge unlocks without a glance at the second name.

NARRATOR (V.O.)
Scrooge never painted out Old Marley's name. There it stood, years afterwards, above the warehouse door. Sometimes people new to the business called Scrooge Scrooge, and sometimes Marley, but he answered to both names. It was all the same to him.

EXT. LONDON STREET - DAY

Fog and bitter cold. SCROOGE edges through the crowd. Passersby part. A beggar half-lifts a hand and drops it. Children do not ask the hour. A blind man's dog tugs its owner into a doorway; the tail wags once, uneasy.

NARRATOR (V.O.)
Oh! But he was a tight-fisted hand at the grind-stone, Scrooge! a squeezing, wrenching, grasping, scraping, clutching, covetous, old sinner! Hard and sharp as flint; secret, and self-contained, and solitary as an oyster. The cold within him froze his old features, nipped his pointed nose, shrivelled his cheek, stiffened his gait; made his eyes red, his thin lips blue; and spoke out shrewdly in his grating voice.

Scrooge does not look back. The city gives him room.

NARRATOR (V.O.)
He carried his own low temperature always about with him. Nobody ever stopped him in the street to say, with gladsome looks, "My dear Scrooge, how are you?" Even the blind men's dogs appeared to know him. But what did Scrooge care! It was the very thing he liked.

INT. SCROOGE AND MARLEY COUNTING-HOUSE - LATE AFTERNOON

Christmas Eve. Cold, bleak, biting; fog at every chink. Candles flare. A very small fire in Scrooge's grate. The door stands open to a dismal little cell beyond.

In the Tank, BOB CRATCHIT copies letters at a desk. His fire is one coal. He glances at the coal-box in Scrooge's room, thinks better of it, winds his long white comforter about him, and tries to warm his hands at the candle flame. It fails.

City clocks have gone three; outside it is already dark. SCROOGE works, unsoftened.

The outer door opens. FRED comes in hard from the fog—ruddy, handsome, eyes bright, breath smoking—cheerful as a stove.

FRED
A merry Christmas, uncle! God save you!

SCROOGE
Bah! Humbug!

FRED
Christmas a humbug, uncle! You don't mean that, I am sure?

SCROOGE
I do. Merry Christmas! What right have you to be merry? What reason have you to be merry? You're poor enough.

FRED
(gaily)
Come, then. What right have you to be dismal? What reason have you to be morose? You're rich enough.

SCROOGE
Bah! Humbug!

FRED
Don't be cross, uncle!

SCROOGE
What else can I be, when I live in such a world of fools as this? Merry Christmas! Out upon merry Christmas! What's Christmas time to you but a time for paying bills without money; a time for finding yourself a year older, but not an hour richer; a time for balancing your books and having every item in 'em through a round dozen of months presented dead against you? If I could work my will, every idiot who goes about with "Merry Christmas" on his lips, should be boiled with his own pudding, and buried with a stake of holly through his heart. He should!

FRED
Uncle!

SCROOGE
Nephew! Keep Christmas in your own way, and let me keep it in mine.

FRED
Keep it! But you don't keep it.

SCROOGE
Let me leave it alone, then. Much good may it do you! Much good it has ever done you!

FRED
There are many things from which I might have derived good, by which I have not profited, I dare say. Christmas among the rest. But I am sure I have always thought of Christmas time, when it has come round—apart from the veneration due to its sacred name and origin, if anything belonging to it can be apart from that—as a good time; a kind, forgiving, charitable, pleasant time; the only time I know of, in the long calendar of the year, when men and women seem by one consent to open their shut-up hearts freely, and to think of people below them as if they really were fellow-passengers to the grave, and not another race of creatures bound on other journeys. And therefore, uncle, though it has never put a scrap of gold or silver in my pocket, I believe that it has done me good, and will do me good; and I say, God bless it!

In the Tank, Cratchit involuntarily applauds—then, horrified, pokes his fire and extinguishes the last frail spark.

SCROOGE
(to Cratchit)
Let me hear another sound from you, and you'll keep your Christmas by losing your situation!
(to Fred)
You're quite a powerful speaker, sir. I wonder you don't go into Parliament.

FRED
Don't be angry, uncle. Come! Dine with us to-morrow.

Scrooge's answer is a look that goes the whole length of a brutal expression.

FRED
But why? Why?

SCROOGE
Why did you get married?

FRED
Because I fell in love.

SCROOGE
(growling)
Because you fell in love! Good afternoon!

FRED
Nay, uncle, but you never came to see me before that happened. Why give it as a reason for not coming now?

SCROOGE
Good afternoon.

FRED
I want nothing from you; I ask nothing of you; why cannot we be friends?

SCROOGE
Good afternoon.

FRED
I am sorry, with all my heart, to find you so resolute. We have never had any quarrel, to which I have been a party. But I have made the trial in homage to Christmas, and I'll keep my Christmas humour to the last. So A Merry Christmas, uncle!

SCROOGE
Good afternoon!

FRED
And A Happy New Year!

SCROOGE
Good afternoon!

Fred leaves without an angry word. At the outer door he bestows the greetings of the season on Cratchit. Cold as he is, Cratchit returns them cordially.

SCROOGE
(muttering)
There's another fellow—my clerk, with fifteen shillings a week, and a wife and family, talking about a merry Christmas. I'll retire to Bedlam.

As Fred goes out, he lets two others in: MR. ALMSLEY and MR. WITHERS—portly, pleasant, hats off, books and papers in hand. They bow.

MR. ALMSLEY
Scrooge and Marley's, I believe. Have I the pleasure of addressing Mr. Scrooge, or Mr. Marley?

SCROOGE
Mr. Marley has been dead these seven years. He died seven years ago, this very night.

MR. ALMSLEY
We have no doubt his liberality is well represented by his surviving partner.

He presents credentials. At the word "liberality," Scrooge frowns, shakes his head, and hands them back.

MR. ALMSLEY
At this festive season of the year, Mr. Scrooge, it is more than usually desirable that we should make some slight provision for the Poor and destitute, who suffer greatly at the present time. Many thousands are in want of common necessaries; hundreds of thousands are in want of common comforts, sir.

SCROOGE
Are there no prisons?

MR. ALMSLEY
Plenty of prisons.

SCROOGE
And the Union workhouses? Are they still in operation?

MR. ALMSLEY
They are. Still. I wish I could say they were not.

SCROOGE
The Treadmill and the Poor Law are in full vigour, then?

MR. ALMSLEY
Both very busy, sir.

SCROOGE
Oh! I was afraid, from what you said at first, that something had occurred to stop them in their useful course. I'm very glad to hear it.

MR. ALMSLEY
Under the impression that they scarcely furnish Christian cheer of mind or body to the multitude, a few of us are endeavouring to raise a fund to buy the Poor some meat and drink, and means of warmth. We choose this time, because it is a time, of all others, when Want is keenly felt, and Abundance rejoices. What shall I put you down for?

SCROOGE
Nothing!

MR. ALMSLEY
You wish to be anonymous?

SCROOGE
I wish to be left alone. Since you ask me what I wish, gentlemen, that is my answer. I don't make merry myself at Christmas and I can't afford to make idle people merry. I help to support the establishments I have mentioned—they cost enough; and those who are badly off must go there.

MR. WITHERS
Many can't go there; and many would rather die.

SCROOGE
If they would rather die, they had better do it, and decrease the surplus population. Besides—excuse me—I don't know that.

MR. ALMSLEY
But you might know it.

SCROOGE
It's not my business. It's enough for a man to understand his own business, and not to interfere with other people's. Mine occupies me constantly. Good afternoon, gentlemen!

They see it is useless and withdraw. Scrooge resumes his labours with an improved opinion of himself.

EXT. COURT OUTSIDE COUNTING-HOUSE - LATE AFTERNOON

Fog and darkness thicken. Link-boys with flaring torches offer to lead carriages. A church tower vanishes into cloud; its bell strikes with a chatter of frozen vibrations. At the corner, labourers' brazier gathers ragged men and boys. Shop windows bright with holly; pale faces pass ruddy in the lamp heat.

A STREET BOY with a scant young nose stoops at Scrooge's keyhole.

STREET BOY
(singing)
God bless you, merry gentleman!
May nothing you dismay!

INT. SCROOGE AND MARLEY COUNTING-HOUSE - CONTINUOUS

Scrooge seizes his ruler with sudden energy. The boy flees; fog and frost reclaim the keyhole.

At last, shutting-up time. Scrooge dismounts his stool with ill will. In the Tank, Cratchit snuffs his candle and puts on his hat.

SCROOGE
You'll want all day to-morrow, I suppose?

BOB CRATCHIT
If quite convenient, sir.

SCROOGE
It's not convenient, and it's not fair. If I was to stop half-a-crown for it, you'd think yourself ill-used, I'll be bound?

Cratchit smiles faintly.

SCROOGE
And yet you don't think me ill-used, when I pay a day's wages for no work.

BOB CRATCHIT
It's only once a year, sir.

SCROOGE
(buttoning his great-coat to the chin)
A poor excuse for picking a man's pocket every twenty-fifth of December! But I suppose you must have the whole day. Be here all the earlier next morning.

Cratchit promises. Scrooge walks out with a growl.

EXT. CORNHILL - EVENING

The office closes in a twinkling. Cratchit, long ends of his white comforter dangling (no great-coat), joins a lane of boys at a slide. He goes down it twenty times, laughing, then runs home toward Camden Town as hard as he can pelt.

INT. MELANCHOLY TAVERN - NIGHT

Scrooge takes his melancholy dinner alone. He reads newspapers. He beguiles the rest of the evening with his banker's-book. No toast. No company.

EXT. SCROOGE'S YARD - NIGHT

A lowering pile of building up a yard—gloomy chambers that once belonged to Marley. Nobody lives in it now but Scrooge; other rooms let out as offices. The yard is so dark that even Scrooge, who knows its every stone, gropes with his hands.

He approaches his door. The knocker—large, old-fashioned—is ordinary iron one moment.

The next: JACOB MARLEY's face.

Not angry or ferocious—looking at Scrooge as Marley used to look: ghostly spectacles turned up on its ghostly forehead. The hair is curiously stirred, as if by breath or hot air; the eyes wide open, motionless; the colour horrible, livid.

Scrooge stares. The apparition becomes a knocker again.

He turns the key, walks in, and lights his candle. He does pause, with a moment's irresolution, before he shuts the door—and he does look cautiously behind it first, as if he half expects to be terrified with the sight of Marley's pigtail sticking out into the hall. But there is nothing.

INT. SCROOGE'S STAIRS AND HALL - NIGHT

He mounts the stairs slowly. Darkness is cheap, and Scrooge likes it. Upstairs, sitting-room, bedroom, lumber-room. All as they should be. Nobody under the table; nobody under the sofa. A small fire in the grate. Nobody under the bed; nobody in the closet; nobody in his dressing-gown, which is hanging up in a suspicious attitude against the wall.

SCROOGE
Humbug!

INT. SCROOGE'S SITTING ROOM - NIGHT

Scrooge takes off his cravat, puts on his dressing-gown and slippers and his nightcap, and sits before the fire to take his gruel.

The fireplace tiles illustrate Scripture stories. As he looks, each tile becomes Marley's face. He gets up restlessly.

A bell in the room begins to swing. Softly at first, then loudly; and every bell in the house joins in.

They cease together. From below: a clanking noise, as if some person were dragging a heavy chain over the casks in the wine-merchant's cellar.

The cellar-door flies open with a booming sound. The chain comes up the stairs—then through the closed door into the room.

JACOB MARLEY stands before him—in his pigtail, usual waistcoat, tights and boots. The chain is clasped about his middle; it winds long and wound about him like a tail—cash-boxes, keys, padlocks, ledgers, deeds, and heavy purses wrought in steel. His body is transparent. Scrooge can see the two buttons on his coat behind.

Marley's face is death-cold; the hair and skirts stir, strangely, as if by hot vapour. A bandage of the best white linen holds his jaw.

SCROOGE
How now! What do you want with me?

MARLEY
Much!

SCROOGE
Who are you?

MARLEY
Ask me who I was.

SCROOGE
Who were you then? You're particular, for a shade.

MARLEY
In life I was your partner, Jacob Marley.

SCROOGE
Can you—can you sit down?

MARLEY
I can.

SCROOGE
Do it, then.

Marley sits in the chair across the fire as if he were quite used to it.

SCROOGE
You don't believe in me.

SCROOGE
I don't.

MARLEY
What evidence would you have of my reality beyond that of your senses?

SCROOGE
I don't know.

MARLEY
Why do you doubt your senses?

SCROOGE
Because a little thing affects them. A slight disorder of the stomach makes them cheats. You may be an undigested bit of beef, a blot of mustard, a crumb of cheese, a fragment of an underdone potato. There's more of gravy than of grave about you, whatever you are!

Scrooge tries to be smart—to keep down his terror.

Marley raises a frightful cry and shakes his chain with such a dismal and appalling noise that Scrooge holds on tight to his chair. The spectre takes the bandage from its head: its lower jaw drops down upon its breast.

SCROOGE
Mercy! Dreadful apparition, why do you trouble me?

MARLEY
Man of the worldly mind! Do you believe in me or not?

SCROOGE
I do. I must. But why do spirits walk the earth, and why do they come to me?

MARLEY
It is required of every man that the spirit within him should walk abroad among his fellowmen, and travel far and wide; and if that spirit goes not forth in life, it is condemned to do so after death. It is doomed to wander through the world—oh, woe is me!—and witness what it cannot share, but might have shared on earth, and turned to happiness!

Again the spectre raises a cry, and shakes its chain and wrings its shadowy hands.

SCROOGE
You are fettered. Tell me why?

MARLEY
I wear the chain I forged in life. I made it link by link, and yard by yard; I girded it on of my own free will, and of my own free will I wore it. Is its pattern strange to you?

Scrooge trembles more and more.

MARLEY
Or would you know the weight and length of the strong coil you bear yourself? It was full as heavy and as long as this, seven Christmas Eves ago. You have laboured on it since. It is a ponderous chain!

SCROOGE
Jacob. Old Jacob Marley, tell me more. Speak comfort to me, Jacob!

MARLEY
I have none to give. It comes from other regions, Ebenezer Scrooge, and is conveyed by other ministers, to other kinds of men. Nor can I tell you what I would. A very little more is all permitted to me. I cannot rest, I cannot stay, I cannot linger anywhere. My spirit never walked beyond our counting-house—mark me!—in life my spirit never roved beyond the narrow limits of our money-changing hole; and weary journeys lie before me!

SCROOGE
You must have been very slow about it, Jacob.

MARLEY
Slow!

SCROOGE
Seven years dead. And travelling all the time!

MARLEY
The whole time. No rest, no peace. Incessant torture of remorse.

SCROOGE
You travel fast?

MARLEY
On the wings of the wind.

SCROOGE
You might have got over a great quantity of ground in seven years.

Marley raises another cry and clanks his chain hideously in the dead silence of the night.

MARLEY
Oh! captive, bound, and double-ironed, not to know that ages of incessant labour, by immortal creatures, for this earth must pass into eternity before the good of which it is susceptible is all developed. Not to know that any Christian spirit working kindly in its little sphere, whatever it may be, will find its mortal life too short for its vast means of usefulness. Not to know that no space of regret can make amends for one life's opportunity misused! Yet such was I! Oh! such was I!

SCROOGE
But you were always a good man of business, Jacob.

MARLEY
Business! Mankind was my business. The common welfare was my business; charity, mercy, forbearance, and benevolence, were, all, my business. The dealings of my trade were but a drop of water in the comprehensive ocean of my business!

He holds up his chain at arm's length and flings it heavily upon the ground again.

MARLEY
At this time of the rolling year I suffer most. Why did I walk through crowds of fellow-beings with my eyes turned down, and never raise them to that blessed Star which led the Wise Men to a poor abode! Were there no poor homes to which its light would have conducted me!

Scrooge is very much dismayed.

MARLEY
Hear me! My time is nearly gone.

SCROOGE
I will. But don't be hard upon me! Don't be flowery, Jacob! Pray!

MARLEY
How it is that I appear before you in a shape that you can see, I may not tell. I have sat invisible beside you many and many a day.

It was not an agreeable idea. Scrooge shivered, and wiped the perspiration from his brow.

MARLEY
That is no light part of my penance. I am here to-night to warn you, that you have yet a chance and hope of escaping my fate. A chance and hope of my procuring, Ebenezer.

SCROOGE
You were always a good friend to me. Thank'ee!

MARLEY
You will be haunted by Three Spirits.

SCROOGE
Is that the chance and hope you mentioned, Jacob?

MARLEY
It is.

SCROOGE
I—I think I'd rather not.

MARLEY
Without their visits you cannot hope to shun the path I tread. Expect the first to-morrow, when the bell tolls One.

SCROOGE
Couldn't I take 'em all at once, and have it over, Jacob?

MARLEY
Expect the second on the next night at the same hour. The third upon the next night when the last stroke of Twelve has ceased to vibrate. Look to see me no more; and look that, for your own sake, you remember what has passed between us!

The spectre takes its bandage from the table and binds its head again. It rises. The air is filled with lamentation and regret from wandering phantoms outside—miserable chained spirits who cry at opportunities of doing good neglected.

Marley walks backward from him; at every step the window raises itself a little, so that when the spectre reaches it, it is wide open. He floats out upon the bleak, dark night.

Scrooge closes the window. He examines the door by which the Ghost had entered—double-locked. He tries to say "Humbug!" but stops at the first syllable. Exhausted, he goes straight to bed, and falls asleep upon the instant.

INT. SCROOGE'S BEDROOM - NIGHT

Scrooge awakens in the dark. The chimes of a neighbouring church strike the four quarters. He listens for the hour.

SCROOGE
The hour itself, and nothing else!

The hour bell sounds—a deep, dull, hollow, melancholy ONE.

Light flashes up in the room upon the instant. The bed-curtains at his face are drawn aside by a hand.

Scrooge starts up half-recumbent—face to face with an unearthly visitor, as close as breath.

THE GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PAST stands there: a strange figure, like a child yet not so like a child as like an old man viewed through some supernatural medium, diminished to a child's proportions. Hair white as if with age hangs about its neck and down its back; the face has not a wrinkle, and the tenderest bloom is on the skin. Long muscular arms and hands; legs and feet bare and delicately formed. A tunic of the purest white; round its waist a lustrous belt that sparkles and shifts. In one hand, a branch of fresh green holly; its dress trimmed with summer flowers. From the crown of its head springs a bright clear jet of light. Under its arm it holds a great extinguisher for a cap.

As Scrooge stares, the figure fluctuates—now one arm, now one leg, now twenty legs, now a head without a body—then is itself again, distinct and clear.

SCROOGE
Are you the Spirit, sir, whose coming was foretold to me?

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PAST
I am!

The voice is soft and gentle. Singularly low, as if at a distance.

SCROOGE
Who, and what are you?

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PAST
I am the Ghost of Christmas Past.

SCROOGE
Long Past?

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PAST
No. Your past.

Scrooge eyes the extinguisher-cap with a special desire to see the Spirit covered.

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PAST
What! Would you so soon put out, with worldly hands, the light I give? Is it not enough that you are one of those whose passions made this cap, and force me through whole trains of years to wear it low upon my brow!

Scrooge reverently disclaims offence. He inquires what business brings the Spirit.

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PAST
Your welfare!

Scrooge mutters that unbroken rest might have been more conducive—

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PAST
Your reclamation, then. Take heed!

It puts out its strong hand and clasps him gently by the arm.

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PAST
Rise! And walk with me!

Scrooge rises. The Spirit makes toward the window. Scrooge clasps his robe in supplication.

SCROOGE
I am a mortal, and liable to fall.

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PAST
Bear but a touch of my hand there—

The Spirit lays its hand upon Scrooge's heart.

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PAST
—and you shall be upheld in more than this!

They pass through the wall.

EXT. COUNTRY ROAD - DAY

Open country road; fields on either hand. Snow upon the ground. Clear, cold winter day. The city has entirely vanished. Darkness and mist gone with it.

Scrooge, still in slippers, dressing-gown, and nightcap, clasps his hands.

SCROOGE
Good Heaven! I was bred in this place. I was a boy here!

A thousand odours float in the air. His lip trembles.

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PAST
Your lip is trembling. And what is that upon your cheek?

SCROOGE
(catching in his voice)
A pimple. Lead me where you will.

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PAST
You recollect the way?

SCROOGE
Remember it! I could walk it blindfold.

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PAST
Strange to have forgotten it for so many years! Let us go on.

They walk. Scrooge recognises every gate, post, and tree. A little market-town appears in the distance—bridge, church, winding river.

EXT. ROAD NEAR MARKET-TOWN - DAY

Shaggy ponies trot toward them with boys upon their backs. Other boys in country gigs and carts, driven by farmers. All in great spirits, shouting Merry Christmas as they part at cross-roads. The crisp air seems to laugh with merry music.

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PAST
These are but shadows of the things that have been. They have no consciousness of us.

Scrooge knows and names them every one. His cold eye glistens. His heart leaps as they pass.

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PAST
The school is not quite deserted. A solitary child, neglected by his friends, is left there still.

Scrooge nods—he knows it—and sobs.

They leave the high-road by a well-remembered lane.

EXT. OLD SCHOOL HOUSE - DAY

A mansion of dull red brick; weathercock-surmounted cupola; a bell hanging in it. Broken fortunes: damp mossy walls, broken windows, decayed gates. Fowls cluck in the stables. Coach-houses overrun with grass.

INT. SCHOOL HALL - DAY

Dreary hall. Open doors show poorly furnished rooms, cold and vast. Earthy savour in the air; chilly bareness.

The Ghost and Scrooge cross to a door at the back. It opens before them.

INT. SCHOOLROOM - DAY

A long, bare, melancholy room; lines of plain deal forms and desks. At one of these, a lonely boy—BOY SCROOGE—reads near a feeble fire.

Grown Scrooge sits down upon a form and weeps to see his poor forgotten self.

Not a latent echo, not a squeak of mice, not a drip from a half-thawed water-spout, not a sigh among leafless boughs, not the idle swinging of an empty store-house door, not a clicking in the fire—but softens him and freer passage to his tears.

The Spirit touches his arm and points to the boy, intent upon his reading.

Outside the window, wonderfully real: a man in foreign garments, axe in his belt, leading an ass laden with wood.

SCROOGE
(in ecstasy)
Why, it's Ali Baba! It's dear old honest Ali Baba! Yes, yes, I know! One Christmas time, when yonder solitary child was left here all alone, he did come, for the first time, just like that. Poor boy! And Valentine—and his wild brother, Orson; there they go! And what's his name, who was put down in his drawers, asleep, at the Gate of Damascus; don't you see him! And the Sultan's Groom turned upside down by the Genii; there he is upon his head! Serve him right. I'm glad of it. What business had he to be married to the Princess!

Scrooge's face is heightened and excited—between laughing and crying.

SCROOGE
There's the Parrot! Green body and yellow tail, with a thing like a lettuce growing out of the top of his head; there he is! Poor Robin Crusoe, he called him, when he came home again after sailing round the island. "Poor Robin Crusoe, where have you been, Robin Crusoe?" The man thought he was dreaming, but he wasn't. It was the Parrot, you know. There goes Friday, running for his life to the little creek! Halloa! Hoop! Halloo!

Then, rapid transition—pity:

SCROOGE
Poor boy!

He cries again. Dries his eyes with his cuff. Puts his hand in his pocket.

SCROOGE
I wish... but it's too late now.

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PAST
What is the matter?

SCROOGE
Nothing. Nothing. There was a boy singing a Christmas Carol at my door last night. I should like to have given him something: that's all.

The Ghost smiles thoughtfully and waves its hand.

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PAST
Let us see another Christmas!

Boy Scrooge grows larger. The room becomes a little darker and more dirty. Panels shrink; windows crack; fragments of plaster fall; naked laths show. He is alone again when all the other boys have gone home for the jolly holidays—not reading now, but walking up and down despairingly.

Scrooge glances anxiously toward the door.

It opens. A little girl, much younger—FAN—comes darting in, puts her arms about the boy's neck, kisses him often.

FAN
Dear, dear brother. I have come to bring you home, dear brother!

She claps her tiny hands, bends down to laugh.

FAN
To bring you home, home, home!

BOY SCROOGE
Home, little Fan?

FAN
Yes! Home, for good and all. Home, for ever and ever. Father is so much kinder than he used to be, that home's like Heaven! He spoke so gently to me one dear night when I was going to bed, that I was not afraid to ask him once more if you might come home; and he said Yes, you should; and sent me in a coach to bring you. And you're to be a man! And are never to come back here; but first, we're to be together all the Christmas long, and have the merriest time in all the world.

BOY SCROOGE
You are quite a woman, little Fan!

She claps and laughs, tries to touch his head, stands on tiptoe to embrace him, drags him toward the door. He goes willingly.

INT. SCHOOL HALL - DAY

A terrible voice:

SCHOOLMASTER (O.S.)
Bring down Master Scrooge's box, there!

The SCHOOLMASTER appears—glares on Master Scrooge with ferocious condescension, shakes hands with him, throws the boy into a dreadful state of mind. He conveys the children toward the best-parlour.

INT. SCHOOL BEST-PARLOUR - DAY

A shivering old well of a room. Maps on the wall; celestial and terrestrial globes in the windows, waxy with cold.

The Schoolmaster produces a decanter of curiously light wine and a block of curiously heavy cake; administers instalments to the young people. A meagre servant is sent out with a glass of "something" for the postboy.

Master Scrooge's trunk is tied on the chaise. The children bid the Schoolmaster good-bye right willingly.

EXT. OLD SCHOOL HOUSE - DAY

They get into the chaise and drive gaily down the garden-sweep. Quick wheels dash hoar-frost and snow from the dark leaves of the evergreens like spray.

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PAST
Always a delicate creature, whom a breath might have withered. But she had a large heart!

SCROOGE
So she had. You're right. I will not gainsay it, Spirit. God forbid!

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PAST
She died a woman, and had, as I think, children.

SCROOGE
One child.

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PAST
True. Your nephew!

Scrooge is uneasy.

SCROOGE
Yes.

EXT. CITY THOROUGHFARES - EVENING

Although they have but that moment left the school, they are now in busy city thoroughfares. Shadowy passengers, carts and coaches. Christmas time again—shops dressed—but evening, streets lighted up.

The Ghost stops at a certain warehouse door.

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PAST
Do you know it?

SCROOGE
Know it! Was I apprenticed here!

INT. FEZZIWIG'S WAREHOUSE - NIGHT

They go in. An old gentleman in a Welsh wig sits behind a desk so high that two inches more stature would knock his head against the ceiling.

SCROOGE
(great excitement)
Why, it's old Fezziwig! Bless his heart; it's Fezziwig alive again!

OLD FEZZIWIG lays down his pen, looks up at the clock—seven. Rubs his hands; adjusts his capacious waistcoat; laughs all over himself from his shoes to his organ of benevolence. Comfortable, oily, rich, fat, jovial voice:

FEZZIWIG
Yo ho, there! Ebenezer! Dick!

Scrooge's former self—YOUNG SCROOGE, now a young man—comes briskly in with his fellow-'prentice, DICK WILKINS.

SCROOGE
(to the Ghost)
Dick Wilkins, to be sure! Bless me, yes. There he is. He was very much attached to me, was Dick. Poor Dick! Dear, dear!

FEZZIWIG
Yo ho, my boys! No more work to-night. Christmas Eve, Dick. Christmas, Ebenezer! Let's have the shutters up before a man can say Jack Robinson!

Sharp clap of his hands.

The two fellows charge into the street with the shutters—one, two, three—up in their places—four, five, six—barred and pinned—seven, eight, nine—back before twelve, panting like race-horses.

FEZZIWIG
Hilli-ho!

He skips down from the high desk with wonderful agility.

FEZZIWIG
Clear away, my lads, and let's have lots of room here! Hilli-ho, Dick! Chirrup, Ebenezer!

Every movable packed off; floor swept and watered; lamps trimmed; fuel heaped upon the fire. The warehouse becomes as snug, warm, dry, and bright a ball-room as one could desire on a winter's night.

In comes a FIDDLER with a music-book; up to the lofty desk; makes an orchestra of it; tunes like fifty stomach-aches.

In comes MRS. FEZZIWIG—one vast substantial smile. In come the three Miss Fezziwigs, beaming. In come six young followers. In come all the young men and women employed in the business. Housemaid with her cousin the baker; cook with the milkman; the boy from over the way hiding behind the girl from next door. In they all come—shy, bold, graceful, awkward—anyhow and everyhow.

Away they go: twenty couple at once; hands half round and back; down the middle and up again; old top couple turning up in the wrong place. Fezziwig claps to the time, walks it, walks it with his dancing partner Mrs. Fezziwig—top couple too, with a good stiff piece of work cut out for them. There were more dances, and there were forfeits, and more dances, and there was cake, and there was negus, and there was a great piece of Cold Roast, and there was a great piece of Cold Boiled, and there were mince-pies, and plenty of beer.

When the clock strikes eleven, the domestic ball breaks up. Mr. and Mrs. Fezziwig take their stations, one on either side of the door, and shaking hands with every person individually as he or she goes out, wish him or her a Merry Christmas.

During the whole of this time, Scrooge has acted like a man out of his wits. His heart and soul are in the scene. He remembers everything, enjoys everything.

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PAST
A small matter to make these silly folks so full of gratitude.

SCROOGE
Small!

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PAST
Why! Is it not? He has spent but a few pounds of your mortal money: three or four perhaps. Is that so much that he deserves this praise?

SCROOGE
It isn't that, Spirit. He has the power to render us happy or unhappy; to make our service light or burdensome; a pleasure or a toil. Say that his power lies in words and looks; in things so slight and insignificant that it is impossible to add and count 'em up: what then? The happiness he gives, is quite as great as if it cost a fortune.

He feels the Spirit's glance, and stops.

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PAST
What is the matter?

SCROOGE
Nothing particular.

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PAST
Something, I think?

SCROOGE
No. No. I should like to be able to say a word or two to my clerk just now. That's all.

The Ghost smiles and waves its hand, saying as it does so—

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PAST
My time grows short. Quick!

INT. A ROOM - EVENING

Another Christmas. Young Scrooge is older now—his face beginning to wear the signs of care and avarice. He sits beside a fair young girl, BELLE, in whose eyes there are tears.

BELLE
It matters little to you, very little. Another idol has displaced me; and if it can cheer and comfort you in time to come, as I would have tried to do, I have no just cause to grieve.

YOUNG SCROOGE
What Idol has displaced you?

BELLE
A golden one.

YOUNG SCROOGE
This is the even-handed dealing of the world! There is nothing on which it is so hard as poverty; and there is nothing it professes to condemn with such severity as the pursuit of wealth!

BELLE
You fear the world too much. All your other hopes have merged into the hope of being beyond the chance of its sordid reproach. I have seen your nobler aspirations fall off one by one, until the master-passion, Gain, engrosses you. Have I not?

YOUNG SCROOGE
What then? Even if I have grown so much wiser, what then? I am not changed towards you.

She shakes her head.

BELLE
Our contract is an old one. It was made when we were both poor and content to be so, until, in good season, we could improve our worldly fortune by our patient industry. You are changed. When it was made, you were another man.

YOUNG SCROOGE
I was a boy.

BELLE
Your own feeling tells you that you were not what you are. I am. That which promised happiness when we were one in heart, is fraught with misery now that we are two. How often and how keenly I have thought of this, I will not say. It is enough that I have thought of it, and can release you.

YOUNG SCROOGE
Have I ever sought release?

BELLE
In words. No. Never.

YOUNG SCROOGE
In what, then?

BELLE
In a changed nature; in an altered spirit; in another atmosphere of life; another Hope as its great end. In everything that made my love of any worth or value in your sight. If this had never been between us, tell me, would you seek me out and try to win me now? Ah, no!

Young Scrooge seems to yield to the justice of this supposition, in spite of himself. But he says, with a struggle—

YOUNG SCROOGE
You think not.

BELLE
I would gladly think otherwise if I could. Heaven knows! When I have learned a Truth like this, I know how strong and irresistible it must be. But if you were free to-day, to-morrow, yesterday, can even I believe that you would choose a dowerless girl—you who, in your very confidence with her, weigh everything by Gain: or, choosing her, if for a moment you were false enough to your one guiding principle to do so, do I not know that your repentance and regret would surely follow? I do; and I release you. With a full heart, for the love of him you once were.

He is about to speak; but with her head turned from him, she resumes.

BELLE
You may—the memory of what is past half makes me hope you will—have pain in this. A very, very brief time, and you will dismiss the recollection of it, gladly, as an unprofitable dream, from which it happened well that you awoke. May you be happy in the life you have chosen!

She leaves him, and they part.

SCROOGE
Spirit! Show me no more! Conduct me home. Why do you delight to torture me?

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PAST
One shadow more!

SCROOGE
No more! No more. I don't wish to see it. Show me no more!

But the relentless Ghost pinions him and forces him to observe.

INT. BELLE'S PARLOUR - NIGHT

A room, not very large or handsome, but full of comfort. Near to the winter fire sits a beautiful young girl—Belle's daughter. Opposite her, the same Belle, now a comely matron, sits beside her daughter.

The door opens. BELLE'S HUSBAND comes in, laden with Christmas toys and presents. The children scramble about him, laughing. He sits down at the fireside with Belle.

BELLE'S HUSBAND
Belle, I saw an old friend of yours this afternoon.

BELLE
Who was it?

BELLE'S HUSBAND
Guess!

BELLE
How can I? Tut, don't I know? Mr. Scrooge.

BELLE'S HUSBAND
Mr. Scrooge it was. I passed his office window; and as it was not shut up, and he had a candle inside, I could scarcely help seeing him. His partner lies upon the point of death, I hear; and there he sat alone. Quite alone in the world, I do believe.

SCROOGE
Spirit! Remove me from this place.

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PAST
I told you these were shadows of the things that have been. That they are what they are, do not blame me!

SCROOGE
Remove me! I cannot bear it!
