He turns upon the Ghost, and in a struggle wrestles with it—seeing that it looks upon him with a face in which fragments of all the faces it has shown lie.

SCROOGE
Leave me! Take me back. Haunt me no longer!

He observes the extinguisher-cap in the Ghost's hand, and with a sudden action presses it down upon the Ghost's head. The Spirit drops beneath it, so that the extinguisher covers its whole form; but though Scrooge presses it down with all his force, he cannot hide the light, which streams from under it in an unbroken flood upon the ground.

He is conscious of being exhausted, and of being overcome by an irresistible drowsiness; and, further, of being in his own bedroom. He has barely time to reel to bed, before he sinks into a heavy sleep.

INT. SCROOGE'S BEDROOM - NIGHT

He wakes in the nick of time to find the bell again upon the stroke of One. No shape appears. He is prepared for almost anything. Five minutes, ten minutes, a quarter of an hour go by, yet nothing comes.

He pulls aside the curtains of his bed—all four—and lies down again, looking up at the wide, heavy bell. Suddenly he remembers Marley's prediction, and gets up softly and shuffles in his slippers to the door.

INT. SCROOGE'S SITTING ROOM - CONTINUOUS

The moment Scrooge's hand is on the lock, a strange voice calls him by his name, and bids him enter. He obeys.

It is his own room—but transformed. The walls and ceiling are so hung with living green that it looks a perfect grove. Bright gleaming berries glisten. The crisp leaves of holly, mistletoe, and ivy reflect the light like mirrors. A mighty blaze goes roaring up the chimney. Heaped on the floor, to form a kind of throne, are turkeys, geese, game, poultry, brawn, great joints of meat, sucking-pigs, long wreaths of sausages, mince-pies, plum-puddings, barrels of oysters, red-hot chestnuts, cherry-cheeked apples, juicy oranges, luscious pears, immense twelfth-cakes, and seething bowls of punch.

Upon this throne sits the GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PRESENT—a jolly Giant, glorious to see; who bears a glowing torch, in shape not unlike Plenty's horn, and holds it up, high up, to shed its light on Scrooge, as he comes peeping round the door.

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PRESENT
Come in! Come in! and know me better, man!

Scrooge enters timidly, hanging his head.

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PRESENT
I am the Ghost of Christmas Present. Look upon me!

The Spirit is clothed in one simple green robe, bordered with white fur. On its head it wears no other covering than a holly wreath, set here and there with shining icicles. Its dark brown curls are long and free; free as its genial face, its sparkling eye, its open hand, its cheery voice, its unconstrained demeanour, and its joyful air. Girded round its middle is an antique scabbard; but no sword is in it, and the ancient sheath is eaten up with rust.

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PRESENT
You have never seen the like of me before!

SCROOGE
Never.

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PRESENT
Have never walked forth with the younger members of my family; meaning (for I am very young) my elder brothers born in these later years?

SCROOGE
I don't think I have. I am afraid I have not. Have you had many brothers, Spirit?

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PRESENT
More than eighteen hundred.

SCROOGE
A tremendous family to provide for! Spirit, conduct me where you will. I went forth last night on compulsion, and I learnt a lesson which is working now. To-night, if you have aught to teach me, let me profit by it.

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PRESENT
Touch my robe!

Scrooge does as he is told, and holds it fast.

EXT. CITY STREETS - CHRISTMAS MORNING

The room, fire, and feast vanish instantly. They stand in the city streets on Christmas morning.

The people shovelling on the housetops are jovial and full of glee, calling out from the parapets and now and then exchanging a facetious snowball—laughing heartily if it goes right and not less heartily if it goes wrong.

The poulterers' shops are still half open. The fruiterers' are radiant: great round pot-bellied baskets of chestnuts loll at the doors; ruddy Spanish Onions shine on the shelves; pears and apples cluster in blooming pyramids; bunches of grapes dangle from hooks; piles of filberts, Norfolk Biffins, oranges and lemons crowd the windows. Gold and silver fish gasp round and round a bowl set among the fruit.

Through the gaps in the Grocers' shutters: scales descend with a merry sound; twine and roller part briskly; canisters rattle; the blended scents of tea and coffee pour into the street. Customers tumble against each other at the door, crash their wicker baskets, leave purchases and run back for them, all in the best humour possible. The Grocer and his people fasten their aprons with polished hearts worn outside.

Steeples begin to call. Good people flock through the streets in their best clothes and gayest faces. From bye-streets and nameless turnings, innumerable poor people carry their dinners toward the bakers' shops.

INT. BAKER'S DOORWAY - DAY

The GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PRESENT stands with SCROOGE beside him in a baker's doorway. He takes off the covers as the bearers pass and sprinkles incense on their dinners from his torch.

Once or twice, when angry words rise between dinner-carriers who have jostled each other, he sheds a few drops of water on them from the torch. Their good humour is restored directly.

DINNER-CARRIER
It was a shame to quarrel upon Christmas Day.

ANOTHER DINNER-CARRIER
And so it was! God love it, so it was!

In time the bells cease. The bakers shut up. A genial shadowing forth of all these dinners remains in the thawed blotch of wet above each baker's oven; the pavement smokes as if its stones were cooking too.

SCROOGE
Is there a peculiar flavour in what you sprinkle from your torch?

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PRESENT
There is. My own.

SCROOGE
Would it apply to any kind of dinner on this day?

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PRESENT
To any kindly given. To a poor one most.

SCROOGE
Why to a poor one most?

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PRESENT
Because it needs it most.

SCROOGE
(after a moment's thought)
Spirit, I wonder you, of all the beings in the many worlds about us, should desire to cramp these people's opportunities of innocent enjoyment.

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PRESENT
I!

SCROOGE
You would deprive them of their means of dining every seventh day, often the only day on which they can be said to dine at all. Wouldn't you?

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PRESENT
I!

SCROOGE
You seek to close these places on the Seventh Day? And it comes to the same thing.

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PRESENT
I seek!

SCROOGE
Forgive me if I am wrong. It has been done in your name, or at least in that of your family.

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PRESENT
There are some upon this earth of yours who lay claim to know us, and who do their deeds of passion, pride, ill-will, hatred, envy, bigotry, and selfishness in our name, who are as strange to us and all our kith and kin, as if they had never lived. Remember that, and charge their doings on themselves, not us.

Scrooge promises that he will. They go on, invisible, into the suburbs. Notwithstanding his gigantic size, the Ghost accommodates himself to any place with ease.

EXT. CRATCHIT DOORSTEP - DAY

The Spirit leads Scrooge straight to a humble four-roomed house. On the threshold he smiles and stops to bless Bob Cratchit's dwelling with a sprinkling of his torch.

INT. CRATCHIT SITTING ROOM - DAY

A poor but brave little room. MRS. CRATCHIT rises—dressed poorly in a twice-turned gown, but brave in ribbons which make a goodly show for sixpence. She lays the cloth, assisted by BELINDA CRATCHIT, second of her daughters, also brave in ribbons.

PETER CRATCHIT plunges a fork into the saucepan of potatoes. The corners of his monstrous shirt collar (Bob's private property, conferred upon his son and heir in honour of the day) get into his mouth; he rejoices to find himself so gallantly attired.

Two smaller Cratchits, LITTLE BOY CRATCHIT and LITTLE GIRL CRATCHIT, come tearing in.

LITTLE BOY CRATCHIT
Outside the baker's we smelt the goose!

LITTLE GIRL CRATCHIT
And knew it for our own!

They dance about the table in luxurious thoughts of sage and onion, and exalt Peter to the skies. Peter, not proud although his collars nearly choke him, blows the fire until the slow potatoes bubble up and knock loudly at the saucepan-lid.

MRS. CRATCHIT
What has ever got your precious father then? And your brother, Tiny Tim! And Martha warn't as late last Christmas Day by half-an-hour?

MARTHA CRATCHIT appears in the doorway, a poor apprentice girl in plain clothes, shawl and bonnet.

MARTHA CRATCHIT
Here's Martha, mother!

LITTLE BOY CRATCHIT
Here's Martha, mother!

LITTLE GIRL CRATCHIT
Hurrah! There's such a goose, Martha!

MRS. CRATCHIT
(kissing her a dozen times, taking off her shawl and bonnet)
Why, bless your heart alive, my dear, how late you are!

MARTHA CRATCHIT
We'd a deal of work to finish up last night, and had to clear away this morning, mother!

MRS. CRATCHIT
Well! Never mind so long as you are come. Sit ye down before the fire, my dear, and have a warm, Lord bless ye!

LITTLE BOY CRATCHIT
No, no! There's father coming!

LITTLE GIRL CRATCHIT
Hide, Martha, hide!

Martha hides behind the closet door.

In comes BOB CRATCHIT—little, threadbare clothes darned up and brushed to look seasonable, at least three feet of comforter exclusive of the fringe hanging down before him—with TINY TIM upon his shoulder. Tiny Tim bears a little crutch; his limbs are supported by an iron frame. He is very small, pale, bright-eyed.

BOB CRATCHIT
(looking round)
Why, where's our Martha?

MRS. CRATCHIT
Not coming.

BOB CRATCHIT
(sudden declension in his high spirits)
Not coming? Not coming upon Christmas Day!

Martha cannot bear it. She comes out prematurely and runs into his arms. The two young Cratchits hustle Tiny Tim off toward the wash-house.

LITTLE BOY CRATCHIT
Come and hear the pudding singing in the copper!

MRS. CRATCHIT
(rallying Bob)
And how did little Tim behave?

BOB CRATCHIT
As good as gold, and better. Somehow he gets thoughtful, sitting by himself so much, and thinks the strangest things you ever heard. He told me, coming home, that he hoped the people saw him in the church, because he was a cripple, and it might be pleasant to them to remember upon Christmas Day, who made lame beggars walk, and blind men see.

Bob's voice is tremulous. It trembles more when he adds—

BOB CRATCHIT
Tiny Tim was growing strong and hearty.

Tiny Tim's active little crutch is heard upon the floor. Back he comes, escorted by his brother and sister, to his stool before the fire.

Bob turns up his cuffs—as if they were capable of being made more shabby—and compounds some hot mixture in a jug with gin and lemons, stirs it round and round, and puts it on the hob to simmer.

Peter and the two young Cratchits go to fetch the goose. They return in high procession.

Such a bustle ensues that a goose might be the rarest of all birds. Mrs. Cratchit makes the gravy (ready beforehand in a little saucepan) hissing hot. Peter mashes the potatoes with incredible vigour. Belinda sweetens up the apple-sauce. Martha dusts the hot plates. Bob takes Tiny Tim beside him in a tiny corner at the table. The two young Cratchits set chairs for everybody, not forgetting themselves, and mount guard upon their posts, cramming spoons into their mouths lest they shriek for goose before their turn.

The dishes are set on. Grace is said. A breathless pause. Mrs. Cratchit looks slowly all along the carving-knife, then plunges it in the breast. The long-expected gush of stuffing issues forth. One murmur of delight arises all round the board. Tiny Tim, excited by the two young Cratchits, beats on the table with the handle of his knife.

TINY TIM
Hurrah!

BOB CRATCHIT
I don't believe there ever was such a goose cooked!

Its tenderness and flavour, size and cheapness, are the themes of universal admiration. Eked out by apple-sauce and mashed potatoes, it is a sufficient dinner for the whole family. Mrs. Cratchit surveys one small atom of a bone upon the dish with great delight.

MRS. CRATCHIT
We haven't ate it all at last!

Yet every one has had enough. The youngest Cratchits in particular are steeped in sage and onion to the eyebrows.

The plates being changed by Belinda, Mrs. Cratchit leaves the room alone—too nervous to bear witnesses—to take the pudding up.

INT. CRATCHIT WASH-HOUSE - DAY

Steam. A smell like a washing-day—that is the cloth. A smell like an eating-house and a pastrycook's next door to each other, with a laundress's next door to that—that is the pudding.

INT. CRATCHIT SITTING ROOM - DAY

In half a minute Mrs. Cratchit enters—flushed, but smiling proudly—with the pudding, like a speckled cannon-ball, so hard and firm, blazing in half of half-a-quartern of ignited brandy, and bedight with Christmas holly stuck into the top.

BOB CRATCHIT
(calmly)
I regard it as the greatest success achieved by Mrs. Cratchit since our marriage.

MRS. CRATCHIT
Now the weight is off my mind, I will confess I had my doubts about the quantity of flour.

Everybody has something to say about it. Nobody says or thinks it is at all a small pudding for a large family. Any Cratchit would blush to hint at such a thing.

At last the dinner is all done, the cloth cleared, the hearth swept, the fire made up. The compound in the jug is tasted and considered perfect. Apples and oranges are put upon the table, and a shovelful of chestnuts on the fire.

All the Cratchit family draw round the hearth in what Bob calls a circle, meaning half a one. At Bob's elbow stands the family display of glass: two tumblers, and a custard-cup without a handle. These hold the hot stuff from the jug as well as golden goblets would have done. Bob serves it out with beaming looks. The chestnuts on the fire sputter and crack noisily.

BOB CRATCHIT
A Merry Christmas to us all, my dears. God bless us!

ALL CRATCHITS
God bless us!

TINY TIM
(the last of all)
God bless us every one!

He sits very close to his father's side upon his little stool. Bob holds his withered little hand as if he loved the child, and wished to keep him by his side, and dreaded that he might be taken from him.

Scrooge, invisible beside the Ghost, watches with an interest he has never felt before.

SCROOGE
Spirit, tell me if Tiny Tim will live.

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PRESENT
I see a vacant seat in the poor chimney-corner, and a crutch without an owner, carefully preserved. If these shadows remain unaltered by the Future, the child will die.

SCROOGE
No, no. Oh, no, kind Spirit! say he will be spared.

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PRESENT
If these shadows remain unaltered by the Future, none other of my race will find him here. What then? If he be like to die, he had better do it, and decrease the surplus population.

Scrooge hangs his head to hear his own words quoted by the Spirit, and is overcome with penitence and grief.

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PRESENT
Man, if man you be in heart, not adamant, forbear that wicked cant until you have discovered What the surplus is, and Where it is. Will you decide what men shall live, what men shall die? It may be, that in the sight of Heaven, you are more worthless and less fit to live than millions like this poor man's child. Oh God! to hear the Insect on the leaf pronouncing on the too much life among his hungry brothers in the dust!

Scrooge bends before the Ghost's rebuke, trembling, eyes upon the ground. He raises them speedily on hearing his own name.

BOB CRATCHIT
Mr. Scrooge! I'll give you Mr. Scrooge, the Founder of the Feast!

MRS. CRATCHIT
The Founder of the Feast indeed! I wish I had him here. I'd give him a piece of my mind to feast upon, and I hope he'd have a good appetite for it.

BOB CRATCHIT
My dear, the children! Christmas Day.

MRS. CRATCHIT
It should be Christmas Day, I am sure, on which one drinks the health of such an odious, stingy, hard, unfeeling man as Mr. Scrooge. You know he is, Robert! Nobody knows it better than you do, poor fellow!

BOB CRATCHIT
My dear. Christmas Day.

MRS. CRATCHIT
I'll drink his health for your sake and the Day's, not for his. Long life to him! A merry Christmas and a happy new year! He'll be very merry and very happy, I have no doubt!

The children drink the toast after her. Tiny Tim drinks it last of all. Bob looks about him gently, as if to find some kinder words; but Scrooge is the Ogre of the family. The mention of his name casts a dark shadow on the party, which is not dispelled for full five minutes.

After it has passed away, they are ten times merrier than before. They sing; they tell stories; Tiny Tim sings, with a plaintive little voice, about a lost child travelling in the snow. Bob holds him close.

By and by they fade, and Scrooge and the Spirit are again upon their travels.

EXT. FRED'S STREET - AFTERNOON

They visit miners in a bleak hut on a moor; lighthouse keepers sharing a Christmas song across the roar of water; then the bright streets of London again. The Ghost's torch sheds light and cheer wherever they go.

At last they stop outside a bright, cheerful house.

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PRESENT
Fred's.

INT. FRED'S DRAWING ROOM - AFTERNOON

Scrooge's nephew FRED and his pretty young wife, and their friends—TOPPER, and the plump sister, and others—are laughing round a table. The room is warm with firelight and good humour.

FRED
He said that Christmas was a humbug, as I live! He believed it too!

FRED'S WIFE
More shame for him, Fred!

FRED
He's a comical old fellow, that's the truth: and not so pleasant as he might be. However, his offences carry their own punishment, and I have nothing to say against him.

FRED'S WIFE
I'm sure he is very rich, Fred. At least you always tell me so.

FRED
What of that, my dear! His wealth is of no use to him. He don't do any good with it. He don't make himself comfortable with it. He hasn't the satisfaction of thinking—ha, ha, ha!—that he is ever going to benefit Us with it.

FRED'S WIFE
I have no patience with him.

SEVERAL GUESTS
Neither have we!

FRED
Oh, I have! I am sorry for him; I couldn't be angry with him if I tried. Who suffers by his ill whims! Himself, always. Here, he takes it into his head to dislike us, and he won't come and dine with us. What's the consequence? He don't lose much of a dinner.

FRED'S WIFE
Indeed, I think he loses a very good dinner.

The company agree heartily.

FRED
I mean to give him the same chance every year, whether he likes it or not, for I pity him. He may rail at Christmas till he dies, but he can't help thinking better of it—I defy him—if he finds me going there, in good temper, year after year, and saying Uncle Scrooge, how are you? If it only puts him in the vein to leave his poor clerk fifty pounds, that's something; and I think I shook him yesterday.

They play at forfeits, at How, When, and Where, and at Blind-man's buff—Topper chasing the plump sister with determined cunning. They sing. They laugh until the house rings.

Scrooge joins in despite himself—softly, invisibly—guessing at the games, begging to stay until the end. The Ghost smiles.

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PRESENT
To the next!

As they leave, Scrooge looks back longingly at the bright room.

EXT. OPEN HEATH AND SEA - NIGHT

The Spirit and Scrooge travel far—over land and sea, to sick beds, foreign lands, almshouses, hospitals, jails. Everywhere the Spirit leaves his blessing, and teaches that Christmas can enter any place.

INT. SCROOGE'S SITTING ROOM - NIGHT

They are again in Scrooge's rooms. It is getting dark. The Ghost has grown older—clearly older. His hair is grey.

SCROOGE
Are spirits' lives so short?

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PRESENT
My life upon this globe, is very brief. It ends to-night.

SCROOGE
To-night!

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PRESENT
To-night at midnight. Hark! The time is drawing near.

The chimes are ringing the three quarters past eleven.

SCROOGE
Forgive me if I am not justified in what I ask, but I see something strange, and not belonging to yourself, protruding from your skirts. Is it a foot or a claw?

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PRESENT
It might be a claw, for the flesh there is upon it. Look here.

From the fold of its robe, it brings two children—wretched, abject, frightful, hideous, miserable. A boy and a girl—yellow, meagre, ragged, scowling, wolfish—but prostrate too in their humility. Where graceful youth should have filled their features out, a stale and shrivelled hand has pinched and twisted them.

SCROOGE
Spirit! are they yours?

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PRESENT
They are Man's. And they cling to me, appealing from their fathers. This boy is Ignorance. This girl is Want. Beware them both, and all of their degree, but most of all beware this boy, for on his brow I see that written which is Doom, unless the writing be erased. Deny it! Slander those who tell it ye! Admit it for your factious purposes, and make it worse. And bide the end!

SCROOGE
Have they no refuge or resource?

GHOST OF CHRISTMAS PRESENT
Are there no prisons? Are there no workhouses?

The bell strikes twelve.

Scrooge looks about him for the Ghost, and sees it not. As the last stroke ceases to vibrate, he remembers the prediction of old Jacob Marley, and lifting up his eyes, beholds a solemn Phantom, draped and hooded, coming, like a mist along the ground, towards him.

EXT. OPEN PLACE - CONTINUOUS

The GHOST OF CHRISTMAS YET TO COME answers not, but points onward with its hand. It is tall, stately, shrouded in a deep black garment that conceals head, face, and form—nothing visible save one outstretched hand.

SCROOGE
You are about to show me shadows of the things that have not happened, but will happen in the time before us. Is that so, Spirit?

The upper portion of the garment contracts for an instant in its folds, as if the Spirit has inclined its head. That is the only answer.

Scrooge's legs tremble. He can hardly stand. The Spirit pauses, observing his condition, giving him time to recover. Behind the dusky shroud, unseen eyes seem intently fixed upon him. He sees nothing but a spectral hand and one great heap of black.

SCROOGE
Ghost of the Future! I fear you more than any spectre I have seen. But as I know your purpose is to do me good, and as I hope to live to be another man from what I was, I am prepared to bear you company, and do it with a thankful heart. Will you not speak to me?

No reply. The hand points straight before them.

SCROOGE
Lead on! Lead on! The night is waning fast, and it is precious time to me, I know. Lead on, Spirit!

The Phantom moves away as it came. Scrooge follows in the shadow of its dress, which bears him up and carries him along.

EXT. THE EXCHANGE - DAY

The city springs up about them. They stand in the heart of it, on 'Change, amongst the merchants—hurrying, chinking money in their pockets, conversing in groups, looking at watches, trifling with great gold seals.

The Spirit stops beside one little knot of business men. The hand points to them. Scrooge advances to listen.

FAT MERCHANT
(great fat man, monstrous chin)
No, I don't know much about it, either way. I only know he's dead.

SECOND MERCHANT
When did he die?

FAT MERCHANT
Last night, I believe.

THIRD MERCHANT
(taking snuff from a vast snuff-box)
Why, what was the matter with him? I thought he'd never die.

FAT MERCHANT
(with a yawn)
God knows.

RED-FACED GENTLEMAN
(pendulous excrescence on his nose that shakes like a turkey-cock's gills)
What has he done with his money?

FAT MERCHANT
(yawning again)
I haven't heard. Left it to his company, perhaps. He hasn't left it to me. That's all I know.

A general laugh.

FAT MERCHANT
It's likely to be a very cheap funeral, for upon my life I don't know of anybody to go to it. Suppose we make up a party and volunteer?

RED-FACED GENTLEMAN
I don't mind going if a lunch is provided. But I must be fed, if I make one.

Another laugh.

FAT MERCHANT
Well, I am the most disinterested among you, after all, for I never wear black gloves, and I never eat lunch. But I'll offer to go, if anybody else will. When I come to think of it, I'm not at all sure that I wasn't his most particular friend; for we used to stop and speak whenever we met. Bye, bye!

Speakers and listeners stroll away and mix with other groups. Scrooge knows the men. He looks toward the Spirit for an explanation. The Phantom glides on.

EXT. CITY STREET - DAY

The Spirit's finger points to two persons meeting. Scrooge knows these men perfectly—wealthy, of great importance. He has always stood well in their esteem, strictly in a business point of view.

FIRST BUSINESSMAN
How are you?

SECOND BUSINESSMAN
How are you?

FIRST BUSINESSMAN
Well! Old Scratch has got his own at last, hey?

SECOND BUSINESSMAN
So I am told. Cold, isn't it?

FIRST BUSINESSMAN
Seasonable for Christmas time. You're not a skater, I suppose?

SECOND BUSINESSMAN
No. No. Something else to think of. Good morning!

Not another word. That is their meeting, their conversation, and their parting.

Scrooge looks about for his own image in his accustomed corner. Another man stands there. The clock points to his usual time of day. He sees no likeness of himself among the multitudes. Quiet and dark, beside him stands the Phantom, with its outstretched hand. Scrooge shudders and feels very cold.

EXT. OBSCURE STREET - NIGHT

They leave the busy scene for an obscure part of the town. The ways are foul and narrow; the shops and houses wretched; the people half-naked, drunken, slipshod, ugly. Alleys and archways disgorge smell, dirt, and life upon the straggling streets. The whole quarter reeks with crime, filth, and misery.

Far in this den stands a low-browed, beetling shop below a pent-house roof—iron, old rags, bottles, bones, and greasy offal bought and sold.

INT. OLD JOE'S SHOP - NIGHT

Upon the floor: heaps of rusty keys, nails, chains, hinges, files, scales, weights, refuse iron. Mountains of unseemly rags, masses of corrupted fat, sepulchres of bones.

OLD JOE, grey-haired, nearly seventy, sits among the wares by a charcoal stove of old bricks, screened from the cold by a frousy curtaining of miscellaneous tatters hung upon a line. He smokes his pipe in calm retirement.

Scrooge and the Phantom enter just as a woman with a heavy bundle slinks in—the CHARWOMAN. Scarcely has she entered when MRS. DILBER, similarly laden, comes in too, closely followed by the UNDERTAKER'S MAN in faded black. All three startle at the sight of each other. After blank astonishment—in which Old Joe joins—they burst into a laugh.

CHARWOMAN
Let the charwoman alone to be the first! Let the laundress alone to be the second; and let the undertaker's man alone to be the third. Look here, old Joe, here's a chance! If we haven't all three met here without meaning it!

OLD JOE
(removing his pipe)
You couldn't have met in a better place. Come into the parlour. You were made free of it long ago, you know; and the other two an't strangers. Stop till I shut the door of the shop. Ah! How it skreeks! There an't such a rusty bit of metal in the place as its own hinges, I believe; and I'm sure there's no such old bones here, as mine. Ha, ha! We're all suitable to our calling, we're well matched. Come into the parlour. Come into the parlour.

The parlour is the space behind the screen of rags. Old Joe rakes the fire with an old stair-rod and trims his smoky lamp with the stem of his pipe.

The Charwoman throws her bundle on the floor and sits on a stool in a flaunting manner, crossing her elbows on her knees, bold defiance at the other two.

CHARWOMAN
What odds then! What odds, Mrs. Dilber? Every person has a right to take care of themselves. He always did.

MRS. DILBER
That's true, indeed! No man more so.

CHARWOMAN
Why then, don't stand staring as if you was afraid, woman; who's the wiser? We're not going to pick holes in each other's coats, I suppose?

MRS. DILBER
No, indeed!

UNDERTAKER'S MAN
We should hope not.

CHARWOMAN
Very well, then! That's enough. Who's the worse for the loss of a few things like these? Not a dead man, I suppose.

MRS. DILBER
(laughing)
No, indeed.

CHARWOMAN
If he wanted to keep 'em after he was dead, a wicked old screw, why wasn't he natural in his lifetime? If he had been, he'd have had somebody to look after him when he was struck with Death, instead of lying gasping out his last there, alone by himself.

MRS. DILBER
It's the truest word that ever was spoke. It's a judgment on him.

CHARWOMAN
I wish it was a little heavier judgment, and it should have been, you may depend upon it, if I could have laid my hands on anything else. Open that bundle, old Joe, and let me know the value of it. Speak out plain. I'm not afraid to be the first, nor afraid for them to see it. We know pretty well that we were helping ourselves, before we met here, I believe. It's no sin. Open the bundle, Joe.

But the man in faded black mounts the breach first and produces his plunder: a seal or two, a pencil-case, a pair of sleeve-buttons, and a brooch of no great value. Old Joe examines and appraises each, chalking sums upon the wall, then adds them up.

OLD JOE
That's your account, and I wouldn't give another sixpence, if I was to be boiled for not doing it. Who's next?

Mrs. Dilber is next. Sheets and towels, a little wearing apparel, two old-fashioned silver teaspoons, a pair of sugar-tongs, and a few boots. Her account goes on the wall.

OLD JOE
I always give too much to ladies. It's a weakness of mine, and that's the way I ruin myself. That's your account. If you asked me for another penny, and made it an open question, I'd repent of being so liberal and knock off half-a-crown.

CHARWOMAN
And now undo my bundle, Joe.

Joe goes down on his knees, unfastens a great many knots, and drags out a large and heavy roll of dark stuff.

OLD JOE
What do you call this? Bed-curtains!

CHARWOMAN
(laughing, leaning forward on her crossed arms)
Ah! Bed-curtains!

OLD JOE
You don't mean to say you took 'em down, rings and all, with him lying there?

CHARWOMAN
Yes I do. Why not?

OLD JOE
You were born to make your fortune, and you'll certainly do it.

CHARWOMAN
(coolly)
I certainly shan't hold my hand, when I can get anything in it by reaching it out, for the sake of such a man as He was, I promise you, Joe. Don't drop that oil upon the blankets, now.

OLD JOE
His blankets?

CHARWOMAN
Whose else's do you think? He isn't likely to take cold without 'em, I dare say.

OLD JOE
(stopping, looking up)
I hope he didn't die of anything catching? Eh?

CHARWOMAN
Don't you be afraid of that. I an't so fond of his company that I'd loiter about him for such things, if he did. Ah! you may look through that shirt till your eyes ache; but you won't find a hole in it, nor a threadbare place. It's the best he had, and a fine one too. They'd have wasted it, if it hadn't been for me.

OLD JOE
What do you call wasting of it?

CHARWOMAN
(with a laugh)
Putting it on him to be buried in, to be sure. Somebody was fool enough to do it, but I took it off again. If calico an't good enough for such a purpose, it isn't good enough for anything. It's quite as becoming to the body. He can't look uglier than he did in that one.

Scrooge listens in horror. Grouped about their spoil in the scanty lamp-light, he views them with detestation and disgust.

Old Joe produces a flannel bag with money and tells out their several gains upon the ground.

CHARWOMAN
Ha, ha! This is the end of it, you see! He frightened every one away from him when he was alive, to profit us when he was dead! Ha, ha, ha!

SCROOGE
(shuddering from head to foot)
Spirit! I see, I see. The case of this unhappy man might be my own. My life tends that way, now. Merciful Heaven, what is this!

INT. DARK BEDCHAMBER - NIGHT

The scene has changed. Scrooge almost touches a bed: a bare, uncurtained bed. Beneath a ragged sheet lies a something covered up which, though dumb, announces itself in awful language.

The room is very dark. A pale light, rising in the outer air, falls straight upon the bed; and on it, plundered and bereft, unwatched, unwept, uncared for, is the body of this man.

The Phantom's steady hand points to the head. The cover is so carelessly adjusted that the slightest raising of it would disclose the face. Scrooge longs to do it, but has no power to withdraw the veil.

A cat tears at the door. There is a sound of gnawing rats beneath the hearth-stone.

SCROOGE
Spirit! this is a fearful place. In leaving it, I shall not leave its lesson, trust me. Let us go!

Still the Ghost points with an unmoved finger to the head.

SCROOGE
I understand you, and I would do it, if I could. But I have not the power, Spirit. I have not the power.

Again it seems to look upon him.

SCROOGE
(agonised)
If there is any person in the town, who feels emotion caused by this man's death, show that person to me, Spirit, I beseech you!

The Phantom spreads its dark robe before him for a moment, like a wing.

INT. DEBTORS' PARLOUR - DAY

Withdrawing the robe reveals a room by daylight. CAROLINE, a mild and patient young mother, walks up and down with anxious eagerness. She starts at every sound, looks out from the window, glances at the clock, tries in vain to work with her needle, and can hardly bear the voices of her CHILDREN in their play.

At length the long-expected knocks. She hurries to the door and meets her husband; a man whose face is careworn and depressed, though he is young. There is a remarkable expression in it now; a kind of serious delight of which he feels ashamed, and which he struggles to repress.

He sits down to the dinner that has been boarding for him by the fire; and when she asks him faintly what news (which is not until after a long silence), he appears embarrassed how to answer.

CAROLINE
Is it good, or bad?

CAROLINE'S HUSBAND
Bad.

CAROLINE
We are quite ruined?

CAROLINE'S HUSBAND
No. There is hope yet, Caroline.

CAROLINE
If he relents, there is! Nothing is past hope, if such a miracle has happened.

CAROLINE'S HUSBAND
He is past relenting. He is dead.

She is a mild and patient creature if her face speaks truth; but she is thankful in her soul to hear it, and she shows it, in the struggle of her features for a moment. Then she prays forgiveness next moment, and is sorry.

CAROLINE'S HUSBAND
To whom will our debt be transferred?

CAROLINE
I don't know. But before that time we shall be ready with the money; and even though we were not, it would be a bad fortune indeed to find so merciless a creditor in his successor. We may sleep to-night with light hearts, Caroline!

Yes. Soften it as they will, their hearts are lighter. The children's faces brighten, and the evening is happier. The only emotion that the Ghost can show Scrooge, caused by the event, is one of pleasure.

SCROOGE
Let me see some tenderness connected with a death, or that dark chamber, Spirit, which we left just now, will be for ever present to me.

The Ghost conducts him through several streets familiar to his feet.

INT. CRATCHIT SITTING ROOM - NIGHT

The house is very quiet. Quiet. Very quiet. The noisy little Cratchits are as still as statues in one corner, and sat looking up at Peter, who has a book before him. The mother and her daughters are engaged in sewing. A chair is set carefully aside—empty—by the fire. Tiny Tim's little crutch leans in the corner.

PETER CRATCHIT
(reading)
"And He took a child, and set him in the midst of them."

The mother lays her work upon the table, and puts her hand up to her face.

MRS. CRATCHIT
The colour hurts my eyes.

The colour? Ah, poor Tiny Tim!

MRS. CRATCHIT
They're better now again. It makes them weak by candle-light; and I wouldn't show weak eyes to your father when he comes home, for the world. It must be near his time.

PETER CRATCHIT
Past it rather. But I think he has walked a little slower than he used, these few last evenings, mother.

They are very quiet again. At last she says, in a steady, cheerful voice, that only falters once:

MRS. CRATCHIT
I have known him walk with—I have known him walk with Tiny Tim upon his shoulder, very fast indeed.

PETER CRATCHIT
And so have I. Often.

LITTLE GIRL CRATCHIT
And so have I.

MRS. CRATCHIT
But he was very light to carry, and his father loved him so, that it was no trouble: no trouble. And there is your father at the door!

She hurries out to meet him. Bob enters—his comforter needed now more than ever. He is a little late. He sits down. They try to help him with tea and comfort.

BOB CRATCHIT
I went there to-day, my dear.

MRS. CRATCHIT
(softly)
The churchyard?

BOB CRATCHIT
Yes. I wish you could have gone. It would have done you good to see how green a place it is. But you'll see it often. I promised him that I would walk there on a Sunday. My little, little child! My little child!

He breaks down. He cannot help it. He leaves the room, and goes upstairs into the room above—which is lighted cheerfully, and hung with Christmas. There is a chair set close beside the child. He kisses the little face. He is reconciled to what has happened, and goes down again quite happy.

They draw about the fire, and talk; the girls and mother working still. Bob tells them of the extraordinary kindness of Mr. Scrooge's nephew, who met him in the street and, seeing he looked a little—"just a little down, you know"—inquired what had happened to distress him.

BOB CRATCHIT
"I am heartily sorry for it, Mr. Cratchit," he said, "and heartily sorry for your good wife." By the bye, how he ever knew that, I don't know.

MRS. CRATCHIT
Knew what, my dear?

BOB CRATCHIT
Why, that you were a good wife.

PETER CRATCHIT
Everybody knows that!

BOB CRATCHIT
"If I can be of service to you in any way," he said, giving me his card, "that's where I live. Pray come to me." Now, it wasn't for the sake of anything he might be able to do for us, so much as for his kind way, that this was quite delightful. It really seemed as if he had known our Tiny Tim, and felt with us.

MRS. CRATCHIT
I'm sure he's a good soul!

BOB CRATCHIT
You would be surer of it, my dear, if you saw and spoke to him. I shouldn't be at all surprised—mark what I say!—if he got Peter a better situation.

MRS. CRATCHIT
Only hear that, Peter.

BOB CRATCHIT
And then Caroline will be a bride! And I know, my dears, that when we recollect how patient and how mild he was—although he was a little, little child—we shall not quarrel easily among ourselves, and forget poor Tiny Tim in doing it.

ALL CRATCHITS
No, never, father!

BOB CRATCHIT
I am very happy. I am very happy!

Scrooge clings to the Spirit's robe.

SCROOGE
Spectre, something informs me that our parting moment is at hand. I know it, but I know not how. Tell me what man that was whom we saw lying dead?

The Ghost of Christmas Yet to Come conveys him, as before.

EXT. CHURCHYARD - NIGHT

A churchyard. Walled in by houses; overrun by grass and weeds, the growth of vegetation's death, not life; choked up with too much burying; fat with repleted appetite.

The Spirit stands among the graves, and points down to One.

Scrooge advances towards it trembling. The Phantom is exactly as it had been, but he dreads that he may see new meaning in its solemn shape.

SCROOGE
Before I draw nearer to that stone to which you point, answer me one question. Are these the shadows of the things that Will be, or are they shadows of things that May be, only?

Still the Ghost points downward to the grave by which it stands.

SCROOGE
Men's courses will foreshadow certain ends, to which, if persevered in, they must lead. But if the courses be departed from, the ends will change. Say it is thus with what you show me!

The Spirit is immovable as ever.

Scrooge creeps towards the grave, trembling as he goes; and following the finger, reads upon the stone of the neglected grave his own name: EBENEZER SCROOGE.

SCROOGE
Am I that man who lay upon the bed?

The finger points from the grave to him, and back again.

SCROOGE
No, Spirit! Oh no, no!

The finger still is there.

SCROOGE
Spirit! Hear me! I am not the man I was. I will not be the man I must have been but for this intercourse. Why show me this, if I am past all hope!

For the first time the hand appears to shake.

SCROOGE
Good Spirit, your nature intercedes for me, and pities me. Assure me that I yet may change these shadows you have shown me, by an altered life!

The kind hand trembles.

SCROOGE
I will honour Christmas in my heart, and try to keep it all the year. I will live in the Past, the Present, and the Future. The Spirits of all Three shall strive within me. I will not shut out the lessons that they teach. Oh, tell me I may sponge away the writing on this stone!

In his agony, he catches the spectral hand. It seeks to free itself, but he is strong in his entreaty, and detains it. The Spirit, stronger yet, repulses him.

Holding up his hands in a last prayer to have his fate reversed, he sees an alteration in the Phantom's hood and dress. It shrinks, collapses, and dwindles down into a bedpost.

INT. SCROOGE'S BEDROOM - MORNING

Yes! and the bedpost was his own. The bed was his own, the room was his own. Best and happiest of all, the Time before him was his own, to make amends in!

He scrambles out of bed.

SCROOGE
I will live in the Past, the Present, and the Future! The Spirits of all Three shall strive within me. Oh Jacob Marley! Heaven, and the Christmas Time be praised for this! I say it on my knees, old Jacob; on my knees!

His face is wet with tears. He folds his dressing-gown about him. The bed-curtains are his own—undisturbed. The bed is his—the room is his.

SCROOGE
They are not torn down. They are not torn down, rings and all. They are here—I am here—the shadows of the things that would have been, may be dispelled. They will be. I know they will!

His hands are busy with his garments; turning them inside out, putting them on upside down, tearing them, mislaying them.

SCROOGE
I don't know what to do! I am as light as a feather, I am as happy as an angel, I am as merry as a schoolboy. I am as giddy as a drunken man. A merry Christmas to everybody! A happy New Year to all the world. Hallo here! Whoop! Hallo!

He has frisked into the sitting-room. The jug without a handle is still there; the saucepan of gruel. The door by which the Ghost of Jacob Marley entered. The corner where the Ghost of Christmas Present sat.

SCROOGE
There's the saucepan that the gruel was in! There's the door, by which the Ghost of Jacob Marley entered! There's the corner where the Ghost of Christmas Present, sat! There's the window where I saw the wandering Spirits! It's all right, it's all true, it all happened. Ha ha ha!

Really, for a man who had been out of practice for so many years, it is a splendid laugh, a most illustrious laugh.

SCROOGE
I don't know what day of the month it is! I don't know how long I've been among the Spirits. I don't know anything. I'm quite a baby. Never mind. I don't care. I'd rather be a baby. Hallo! Whoop! Hallo here!

He rushes to the window, opens it, and puts out his glowing head. No fog, no mist; clear, bright, jovial, stirring cold; golden sunlight; heavenly sky; sweet fresh air; merry bells. Oh, glorious! Glorious!

SCROOGE
(calling down)
What's to-day?

A BOY IN SUNDAY CLOTHES in the street below looks up.

BOY IN SUNDAY CLOTHES
Eh?

SCROOGE
What's to-day, my fine fellow?

BOY IN SUNDAY CLOTHES
To-day! Why, Christmas Day.

SCROOGE
It's Christmas Day! I haven't missed it. The Spirits have done it all in one night. They can do anything they like. Of course they can. Of course they can. Hallo, my fine fellow!

BOY IN SUNDAY CLOTHES
Hallo!

SCROOGE
Do you know the Poulterer's, in the next street but one, at the corner?

BOY IN SUNDAY CLOTHES
I should hope I did.

SCROOGE
An intelligent boy! A remarkable boy! Do you know whether they've sold the prize Turkey that was hanging up there?—Not the little prize Turkey: the big one?

BOY IN SUNDAY CLOTHES
What, the one as big as me?

SCROOGE
What a delightful boy! It's a pleasure to talk to him. Yes, my buck!

BOY IN SUNDAY CLOTHES
It's hanging there now.

SCROOGE
Is it? Go and buy it.

BOY IN SUNDAY CLOTHES
Walk-er!

SCROOGE
No, no, I am in earnest. Go and buy it, and tell 'em to bring it here, that I may give them the direction where to take it. Come back with the man, and I'll give you a shilling. Come back with him in less than five minutes and I'll give you half-a-crown!

The boy is off like a shot.

SCROOGE
I'll send it to Bob Cratchit's! He sha'n't know who sends it. It's twice the size of Tiny Tim. Joe Miller never made such a joke as sending it to Bob's will be!

He chuckles, dresses in his best, shaves—sometimes starting and laughing again—and goes out into the street.

EXT. LONDON STREETS - CHRISTMAS MORNING

People are by this time pouring forth. Scrooge regards every one with a delighted smile. He looks so pleasant that three or four good-humoured fellows say, "Good morning, sir! A merry Christmas to you!" And Scrooge says often afterwards, that of all the blithe sounds he has ever heard, those are the blithest in his ears.

He has not gone far, when coming on towards him he beholds the portly gentleman who walked into his counting-house the day before—MR. ALMSLEY—and said, "Scrooge and Marley's, I believe?"

SCROOGE
My dear sir. How do you do? I hope you succeeded yesterday. It was very kind of you. A merry Christmas to you, sir!

MR. ALMSLEY
Mr. Scrooge?

SCROOGE
Yes. That is my name, and I fear it may not be pleasant to you. Allow me to ask your pardon. And will you have the goodness—

Scrooge whispers in his ear.

MR. ALMSLEY
Lord bless me! My dear Mr. Scrooge, are you serious?

SCROOGE
If you please. Not a farthing less. A great many back-payments are included in it, I assure you. Will you do me that favour?

MR. ALMSLEY
My dear sir, I don't know what to say to such munifi—

SCROOGE
Don't say anything, please. Come and see me. Will you come and see me?

MR. ALMSLEY
I will!

SCROOGE
Thank'ee. I am much obliged to you. I thank you fifty times. Bless you!

He goes to church, and walks the streets, and watches the people hurrying to and fro, and pats children on the head, and questions beggars, and looks down into the kitchens of houses, and up to the windows, and finds that everything could yield him pleasure. He had never dreamed that any walk—that anything—could give him so much happiness.

In the afternoon he turns his steps towards his nephew's house.

INT. FRED'S DRAWING ROOM - AFTERNOON

He passes the door a dozen times, before he has the courage to go up and knock. But he makes a dash, and does it.

FRED
(opening)
Why bless my soul! Who's that?

SCROOGE
It's I. Your uncle Scrooge. I have come to dinner. Will you let me in, Fred?

Let him in! It is a mercy he doesn't shake his arm off. He feels at home in five minutes. Nothing could be heartier. His niece looks just the same. So does Topper when he comes. So do the plump sister when she comes. So does every one when they come. Wonderful party, wonderful games, wonderful unanimity, wonderful happiness!

INT. SCROOGE AND MARLEY COUNTING-HOUSE - MORNING

He is early at the office next morning. Oh, he is early there. If he can only be there first, and catch Bob Cratchit coming late! That is the thing he has set his heart upon.

And he does it; yes, he does! The clock strikes nine. No Bob. A quarter past. No Bob. He is full eighteen minutes and a half behind his time. Scrooge sits with his door wide open, that he may see him come into the Tank.

Bob's hat is off before he opens the door; his comforter too. He is on his stool in a jiffy; driving away with his pen, as if he were trying to overtake nine o'clock.

SCROOGE
Hallo! What do you mean by coming here at this time of day?

BOB CRATCHIT
I am very sorry, sir. I am behind my time.

SCROOGE
You are? Yes. I think you are. Step this way, sir, if you please.

BOB CRATCHIT
It's only once a year, sir. It shall not be repeated. I was making rather merry yesterday, sir.

SCROOGE
Now, I'll tell you what, my friend. I am not going to stand this sort of thing any longer. And therefore—

He leaps from his stool, and gives Bob such a dig in the waistcoat that he staggers back into the Tank—

SCROOGE
—and therefore I am about to raise your salary!

Bob trembles, and gets a little nearer to the ruler. He has a momentary idea of knocking Scrooge down with it, holding him, and calling to the people in the court for help and a strait-waistcoat.

SCROOGE
A merry Christmas, Bob! A merrier Christmas, Bob, my good fellow, than I have given you for many a year! I'll raise your salary, and endeavour to assist your struggling family, and we will discuss your affairs this very afternoon, over a Christmas bowl of smoking bishop, Bob! Make up the fires, and buy another coal-scuttle before you dot another i, Bob Cratchit!

Bob shakes both his hands at once, half laughing, half crying.

EXT. LONDON STREETS - DAY

Snow lies clean on the cobbles. Months have softened into a bright winter morning. SCROOGE, older coat brushed, face open and alive, walks among the PASSERSBY with a step that belongs to the living. He lifts his hat. He greets a child. He stops a beggar with a coin and a clasp of the hand.

NARRATOR (V.O.)
Scrooge was better than his word. He did it all, and infinitely more.

A knot of merchants watch him pass. One chuckles behind a glove. Scrooge only smiles wider and goes on.

NARRATOR (V.O.)
Some people laughed to see the alteration in him, but he let them laugh, and little heeded them; for he was wise enough to know that nothing ever happened on this globe, for good, at which some people did not have their fill of laughter in the outset. His own heart laughed: and that was quite enough for him.

INT. CRATCHIT HOME - DAY

A small room made warm by a good fire and a second coal-scuttle. BOB CRATCHIT at the table; MRS. CRATCHIT laying bread; PETER CRATCHIT, MARTHA CRATCHIT, LITTLE GIRL CRATCHIT, LITTLE BOY CRATCHIT crowded near. TINY TIM, living, bright-eyed, his little crutch against the chair, sits upon SCROOGE'S knee as upon a second father's.

Scrooge steadies the boy. Tim leans into him without fear.

NARRATOR (V.O.)
And to Tiny Tim, who did NOT die, he was a second father. He became as good a friend, as good a master, and as good a man, as the good old city knew, or any other good old city, town, or borough, in the good old world.

Bob meets his wife's eyes across the room. Both shine.

EXT. LONDON STREETS - CHRISTMAS MORNING

Church bells. SCROOGE moves through the throng, wishing strangers joy, stopping to watch carolers, pressing money into MR. ALMSLEY's hand with both of his when their paths cross again. No Spirit walks beside him. Only daylight, and the city, and his own changed gait.

NARRATOR (V.O.)
He had no further intercourse with Spirits, but lived upon the Total Abstinence Principle, ever afterwards; and it was always said of him, that he knew how to keep Christmas well, if any man alive possessed the knowledge.

INT. CRATCHIT HOME - CHRISTMAS DAY

The Cratchit table is fuller than before. SCROOGE at its head as welcome guest; BOB CRATCHIT carving; MRS. CRATCHIT flushed with pride; the children noisy and glad. TINY TIM raised in Bob's arms for the toast, crutch tucked close.

NARRATOR (V.O.)
May that be truly said of us, and all of us!

TINY TIM
God bless us, every one!

Scrooge's face breaks into pure delight. The family echoes the boy. Outside the window, snow falls soft on London.

FADE OUT.

THE END
